people.name: Trajan
people.summary: Mentioned alongside Augustus as one of only two emperors who added more territory to the empire than Justinian, serving as the ultimate benchmark for imperial conquest.

summaries_by_episode.9: Mentioned alongside Augustus as one of only two emperors who added more territory to the empire than Justinian, serving as the ultimate benchmark for imperial conquest.

people.name: Hadrian
people.summary: Mentioned alongside Pompey as a historical predecessor in whose footsteps Julian was following when he left Antioch for the Persian campaign.

summaries_by_episode.5: Mentioned alongside Pompey as a historical predecessor in whose footsteps Julian was following when he left Antioch for the Persian campaign.

people.name: The Five Good Emperors
people.summary: Brownworth praises them as the best solution Rome found to the succession problem: each adopted and trained a capable successor during his own lifetime. The system worked brilliantly until Marcus Aurelius broke with it by leaving the throne to his own son Commodus.

summaries_by_episode.2: Brownworth praises them as the best solution Rome found to the succession problem: each adopted and trained a capable successor during his own lifetime. The system worked brilliantly until Marcus Aurelius broke with it by leaving the throne to his own son Commodus.

people.role: Sequence of Roman Emperors (Nerva, Trajan, Hadrian, Antoninus Pius, Marcus Aurelius)

people.name: Marcus Aurelius
people.summary: Called the 'Philosopher Emperor' by Brownworth, he was the last and most famous of the Five Good Emperors. He broke the adoptive succession by leaving the empire to his own son Commodus rather than a trained heir — an understandable sentiment, Brownworth says, but one Rome would pay dearly for.

summaries_by_episode.2: Called the 'Philosopher Emperor' by Brownworth, he was the last and most famous of the Five Good Emperors. He broke the adoptive succession by leaving the empire to his own son Commodus rather than a trained heir — an understandable sentiment, Brownworth says, but one Rome would pay dearly for.

people.name: Tertullian
people.summary: Brownworth quotes Tertullian's second-century challenge 'What does Jerusalem have to do with Athens?' to frame the perennial tension between faith and reason, arguing that the West chose one or the other while Byzantium uniquely sustained both simultaneously.

summaries_by_episode.17: Brownworth quotes Tertullian's second-century challenge 'What does Jerusalem have to do with Athens?' to frame the perennial tension between faith and reason, arguing that the West chose one or the other while Byzantium uniquely sustained both simultaneously.

people.name: Commodus
people.summary: Son of Marcus Aurelius and the emperor whose erratic reign Brownworth marks as the start of Rome's steady decline. Genuinely believing himself a reincarnated Hercules, he fought gladiators and animals in the Colosseum, claimed 12,000 arena kills, renamed Rome 'Colonia Commodiana,' replaced all calendar months with versions of his own name, and was eventually assassinated after a 12-year reign.

summaries_by_episode.2: Son of Marcus Aurelius and the emperor whose erratic reign Brownworth marks as the start of Rome's steady decline. Genuinely believing himself a reincarnated Hercules, he fought gladiators and animals in the Colosseum, claimed 12,000 arena kills, renamed Rome 'Colonia Commodiana,' replaced all calendar months with versions of his own name, and was eventually assassinated after a 12-year reign.

people.name: Carus
people.summary: The emperor immediately preceding Diocletian's rise. Brownworth calls him 'the emperor of the moment' in 283 — a former head of the praetorian guard who had seized the throne from his predecessor. He was killed by a bolt of lightning on campaign against Persia, which Brownworth suggests was probably Diocletian's doing.

summaries_by_episode.2: The emperor immediately preceding Diocletian's rise. Brownworth calls him 'the emperor of the moment' in 283 — a former head of the praetorian guard who had seized the throne from his predecessor. He was killed by a bolt of lightning on campaign against Persia, which Brownworth suggests was probably Diocletian's doing.

people.name: Diocletian
people.summary: Born of humble origins as Gaius Valerius Diocles, son of a freedman and scribe, he rose through the army to command the imperial bodyguard and seized power in 284 after accusing and executing Aper. He reformed the army, tax system, and imperial succession, most strikingly by voluntarily retiring in 305 — a decision Brownworth calls his most surprising and enduring contribution.

summaries_by_episode.1: Brownworth names Diocletian as his chosen starting point for Byzantine history, describing him as the brilliant, ambitious emperor who split the empire into eastern and western halves in 285 AD and then, remarkably, walked away from power. He is placed in the 'troubled third century' and will be the subject of the next episode.
summaries_by_episode.2: Born of humble origins as Gaius Valerius Diocles, son of a freedman and scribe, he rose through the army to command the imperial bodyguard and seized power in 284 after accusing and executing Aper. He reformed the army, tax system, and imperial succession, most strikingly by voluntarily retiring in 305 — a decision Brownworth calls his most surprising and enduring contribution.
summaries_by_episode.3: Brownworth refers to Diocletian as the 'grandfather of Byzantium' and notes his surprising abdication. His Tetrarchy system, intended to bring peace and prosperity, instead degenerated into civil war. He was called out of retirement to try to stabilize the empire with a new official tetrarchy, but even that solution did not survive the year.
summaries_by_episode.4: Referenced as the architect of the governing system that Constantine's death ultimately ended. Constantine left no succession plan, and his death signaled the final collapse of Diocletian's carefully constructed imperial framework.

people.summary_synthesized: A Roman emperor of humble origins who rose through the military ranks to seize power in 284, Diocletian reorganized the empire's army, tax system, and governance, most notably by dividing imperial authority through the Tetrarchy and splitting the empire into eastern and western halves. His voluntary abdication in 305 was without precedent, though the system he designed soon collapsed into civil war, and even his emergence from retirement to broker a new arrangement failed to restore stability. Considered the foundational architect of what would become the Byzantine state, his carefully constructed imperial framework ultimately dissolved in the power struggles following Constantine's death.

people.name: Helena
people.summary: Constantine's mother, who at age 72 set out to the Holy Land, founding churches along the way and discovering the True Cross. She built the Church of the Nativity at Bethlehem, one on the Mount of Olives, and the Church of the Holy Sepulchre at Jerusalem. She died in the Holy Land, remembered as the first recorded Christian pilgrim.

summaries_by_episode.4: Constantine's mother, who at age 72 set out to the Holy Land, founding churches along the way and discovering the True Cross. She built the Church of the Nativity at Bethlehem, one on the Mount of Olives, and the Church of the Holy Sepulchre at Jerusalem. She died in the Holy Land, remembered as the first recorded Christian pilgrim.

people.name: Carinus
people.summary: Older son of Carus, described by Brownworth as a bit of a playboy but a good general. Left as Augustus of the West while his father campaigned in the east, he crushed German and British revolts and an Italian pretender before being killed by one of his own officers — whose wife Carinus had seduced — at the Battle of the River Margus.

summaries_by_episode.2: Older son of Carus, described by Brownworth as a bit of a playboy but a good general. Left as Augustus of the West while his father campaigned in the east, he crushed German and British revolts and an Italian pretender before being killed by one of his own officers — whose wife Carinus had seduced — at the Battle of the River Margus.

people.name: Constantius Chlorus
people.summary: Constantine's father and Augustus of the West under the Tetrarchy. His nickname 'Chlorus' (Pale) reflected his declining health — he was likely dying from leukemia and unable to prevent Galerius from installing his own candidates. He died in York the spring after his campaign in Britain against the Picts, having just earned the title Britannicus Maximus.

summaries_by_episode.2: Son-in-law of Maximian and one of the two men raised to Caesar in the Tetrarchy of 293. Brownworth mentions him in the context of completing the four-man ruling structure, and notes at the episode's close that his son Constantine would one day reunite the empire.
summaries_by_episode.3: Constantine's father and Augustus of the West under the Tetrarchy. His nickname 'Chlorus' (Pale) reflected his declining health — he was likely dying from leukemia and unable to prevent Galerius from installing his own candidates. He died in York the spring after his campaign in Britain against the Picts, having just earned the title Britannicus Maximus.

people.summary_synthesized: Constantius Chlorus served as Caesar and later Augustus of the West under the Tetrarchy established in 293, making him one of the four co-rulers of the Roman Empire. His nickname "Chlorus," meaning "Pale," reflected a declining health attributed to leukemia, which left him unable to counter Galerius's political maneuvering. He died in York shortly after a successful campaign against the Picts in Britain, having earned the title Britannicus Maximus; his son Constantine would go on to reunite the empire.

people.name: Lactantius
people.summary: A historian cited by Brownworth as the source for Constantine's famous vision before the Battle of the Milvian Bridge, probably writing one or two years after the actual event. Lactantius records that the vision consisted of a private dream telling Constantine to mark his soldiers with the chi-rho, the first two letters of Christ's name in Greek.

summaries_by_episode.3: A historian cited by Brownworth as the source for Constantine's famous vision before the Battle of the Milvian Bridge, probably writing one or two years after the actual event. Lactantius records that the vision consisted of a private dream telling Constantine to mark his soldiers with the chi-rho, the first two letters of Christ's name in Greek.

people.name: Maximian
people.summary: Diocletian's old co-emperor who 'never really wanted to retire in the first place.' He was repeatedly called back to power — first by his son Maxentius, then on his own initiative — and repeatedly failed. After attempting to overthrow his own son and then fleeing to Constantine, his son-in-law, he eventually declared Constantine dead and proclaimed himself emperor, only to be cornered at Marseilles and forced to commit suicide.

summaries_by_episode.2: Diocletian's drinking buddy and adopted colleague, elevated first to Caesar then to Augustus of the West to help govern the empire. Brownworth notes he was honest and hardworking but most crucially willing to accept second place to Diocletian, and loyally — if reluctantly — retired alongside him in 305.
summaries_by_episode.3: Diocletian's old co-emperor who 'never really wanted to retire in the first place.' He was repeatedly called back to power — first by his son Maxentius, then on his own initiative — and repeatedly failed. After attempting to overthrow his own son and then fleeing to Constantine, his son-in-law, he eventually declared Constantine dead and proclaimed himself emperor, only to be cornered at Marseilles and forced to commit suicide.

people.summary_synthesized: Maximian was elevated by Diocletian—first to Caesar, then to Augustus of the West—to share the burden of governing the empire, and loyally if reluctantly retired alongside him in 305. He never reconciled himself to private life, however, and was drawn back into politics repeatedly: first by his son Maxentius, then on his own initiative, launching a series of failed bids for power. After turning against both his son and his son-in-law Constantine, he declared Constantine dead and proclaimed himself emperor, only to be cornered at Marseilles and forced to commit suicide.

people.name: Anthony the Great
people.summary: The famous Egyptian ascetic who led the people of Alexandria in rioting against the appointment of Arius, forcing Constantine to back off his decision and recall Arius for further inquiry.

summaries_by_episode.4: The famous Egyptian ascetic who led the people of Alexandria in rioting against the appointment of Arius, forcing Constantine to back off his decision and recall Arius for further inquiry.

people.name: Numerian
people.summary: Younger son of Carus, described by Brownworth as a decent poet but not much else. Son-in-law of Aper, he took command of the eastern expedition after his father's death but was murdered — found partially decomposed in his closed carriage by the time the army reached Nicomedia — with Diocletian blaming and executing Aper for the crime.

summaries_by_episode.2: Younger son of Carus, described by Brownworth as a decent poet but not much else. Son-in-law of Aper, he took command of the eastern expedition after his father's death but was murdered — found partially decomposed in his closed carriage by the time the army reached Nicomedia — with Diocletian blaming and executing Aper for the crime.

people.role: Roman Emperor (Caesar, then de facto Augustus of the East)

people.name: Arius
people.summary: A young presbyter of Alexandria who around 320 threatened to permanently split the Christian world with his doctrine that God the Father had created Christ, making Christ a perfect man but not God. Condemned at the Council of Nicaea, he was later reinstated by Constantine but died in gruesome circumstances before fully returning to power.

summaries_by_episode.4: A young presbyter of Alexandria who around 320 threatened to permanently split the Christian world with his doctrine that God the Father had created Christ, making Christ a perfect man but not God. Condemned at the Council of Nicaea, he was later reinstated by Constantine but died in gruesome circumstances before fully returning to power.

people.name: Galerius
people.summary: The eastern Augustus who despised Constantius Chlorus and dominated the early Tetrarchy. He incited Roman hatred by trying to tax the city of Rome itself, and his attempts to crush Maxentius failed when his army succumbed to bribes. By the end he was hopelessly obese and dying from a horrendous cancer; in agony, he suspended all persecutions of Christians and ordered churches built and prayers said on his behalf, but did not survive to hear them.

summaries_by_episode.2: Son-in-law of Diocletian, raised to the rank of Caesar as part of the Tetrarchy established in 293. Brownworth mentions him as one of the two caesars appointed to complete the four-man ruling structure, and notes that after Diocletian's retirement Galerius was among the successors who fell to fighting.
summaries_by_episode.3: The eastern Augustus who despised Constantius Chlorus and dominated the early Tetrarchy. He incited Roman hatred by trying to tax the city of Rome itself, and his attempts to crush Maxentius failed when his army succumbed to bribes. By the end he was hopelessly obese and dying from a horrendous cancer; in agony, he suspended all persecutions of Christians and ordered churches built and prayers said on his behalf, but did not survive to hear them.

people.summary_synthesized: Raised to the rank of Caesar under Diocletian's Tetrarchy in 293, Galerius rose to become the dominant eastern Augustus following Diocletian's retirement, though his reign was marked by overreach and failure. His attempt to tax the city of Rome inflamed public hatred, and his campaign against the usurper Maxentius collapsed when his troops accepted bribes. He died in agony from a severe illness, but not before issuing an edict suspending the persecution of Christians and requesting their prayers on his behalf—a reversal he did not live to see answered.

people.name: Licinius
people.summary: Picked as the new Augustus of the West by Diocletian at the conference of emperors, Licinius survived and eventually controlled the East. He joined Constantine in issuing the Edict of Milan and sealed their alliance with the marriage of Constantine's sister Constantia. Despite being at best a lukewarm supporter of Christianity, he became increasingly suspicious of Christians as a fifth column, executed bishops, and destroyed churches — giving Constantine the pretext to invade and ultimately defeat him. He surrendered on condition his life be spared, was sent into comfortable exile, and was then hanged months later.

summaries_by_episode.3: Picked as the new Augustus of the West by Diocletian at the conference of emperors, Licinius survived and eventually controlled the East. He joined Constantine in issuing the Edict of Milan and sealed their alliance with the marriage of Constantine's sister Constantia. Despite being at best a lukewarm supporter of Christianity, he became increasingly suspicious of Christians as a fifth column, executed bishops, and destroyed churches — giving Constantine the pretext to invade and ultimately defeat him. He surrendered on condition his life be spared, was sent into comfortable exile, and was then hanged months later.
summaries_by_episode.4: The eastern emperor who was defeated and killed by Constantine by 323. He had earlier given warm welcome to Arius in Nicomedia, and a synod called by the local bishop there endorsed Arius's teachings.

people.summary_synthesized: Roman emperor who rose from Diocletian's conference of emperors to control the eastern half of the empire, Licinius co-issued the Edict of Milan with Constantine and cemented their alliance through marriage to Constantine's sister Constantia. Though at best a lukewarm supporter of Christianity, he grew suspicious of Christians as a potential fifth column, executing bishops and destroying churches, and also gave welcome to Arius in Nicomedia, where a local synod endorsed Arian teachings. His actions gave Constantine a pretext to invade; defeated by 323, he surrendered on a promise of clemency, was exiled in comfort, and was subsequently hanged.

people.name: Maximinus Daia
people.summary: The Caesar of the East under Diocletian's reorganized Tetrarchy, and a zealous pagan who vowed to eradicate Christianity if the gods gave him victory. He invaded Licinius's territory with 70,000 troops and seized Byzantium, but was soundly defeated despite his numerical advantage. Disguised as a slave he fled but chose a slow poison to commit suicide, which drove him mad — he ate handfuls of dirt and took four agonizing days to die.

summaries_by_episode.3: The Caesar of the East under Diocletian's reorganized Tetrarchy, and a zealous pagan who vowed to eradicate Christianity if the gods gave him victory. He invaded Licinius's territory with 70,000 troops and seized Byzantium, but was soundly defeated despite his numerical advantage. Disguised as a slave he fled but chose a slow poison to commit suicide, which drove him mad — he ate handfuls of dirt and took four agonizing days to die.

people.role: Roman Emperor (Caesar, then Augustus of the East)

people.name: Constantine the Great
people.summary: Brownworth calls Constantine the 'father of Byzantium,' saying seldom has one man had such an effect on history. He inherited a chaotic tetrarchic empire, fought his way to sole rule through a series of civil wars, and transformed it into a Christian, ordered state with himself as absolute ruler appointed by God. His conversion, dated by him to the Battle of the Milvian Bridge, marked a watershed moment in history.

summaries_by_episode.1: Brownworth identifies Constantine as the conventional starting point of the Byzantine Empire, crediting him with refounding the city of Byzantium as Constantinople to serve as a new Christian imperial capital. He notes that Constantine himself was undeniably Roman, standing in an unbroken line of emperors from Augustus, which makes labeling him the founder of a 'Byzantine' empire complicated.
summaries_by_episode.2: Mentioned only in the closing teaser as the subject of the next episode. Brownworth describes him as an ignored son who would one day rise to reunite the empire and become the only emperor ever called 'the Great.'
summaries_by_episode.3: Brownworth calls Constantine the 'father of Byzantium,' saying seldom has one man had such an effect on history. He inherited a chaotic tetrarchic empire, fought his way to sole rule through a series of civil wars, and transformed it into a Christian, ordered state with himself as absolute ruler appointed by God. His conversion, dated by him to the Battle of the Milvian Bridge, marked a watershed moment in history.
summaries_by_episode.4: Having conquered the Roman Empire, Constantine faced the harder task of unifying it under one church. He presided over the Council of Nicaea, founded Constantinople as the new Rome, and died after a 31-year reign — the longest since Augustus — having been baptized at the very last moment by an Arian bishop.
summaries_by_episode.5: A driving personality of history whose death left chaos in its wake. Brownworth notes his arrogance and lack of imagination in naming his children, and that he initiated a Persian war that still had to be fought after his death.
summaries_by_episode.6: Mentioned as the progenitor of the dynasty that ended with Julian the Apostate, who was the last male relative of Constantine.
summaries_by_episode.8: Constantine is mentioned briefly as the benchmark for monumental building campaigns — Justinian's post-Nika building program was 'the likes of which the empire had not seen since the days of Constantine.'
summaries_by_episode.9: Mentioned as the builder of the original Hagia Sophia — described as a small structure that lasted only 44 years before being destroyed in a riot. Also invoked via Justinian's boast "Solomon, I have surpassed thee," which echoes Constantine's own founding legend.
summaries_by_episode.10: Mentioned at the episode's close as the emperor next to whom Heraclius was buried in the Church of the Holy Apostles. Brownworth implies the pairing is fitting — both were epoch-defining figures who saved the empire at critical moments.
summaries_by_episode.12: Referenced as the founder of the Church of the Holy Apostles, the traditional burial place of the emperors, which Justinian later rebuilt and Basil I restored again.
summaries_by_episode.16: Brownworth invokes Constantine the Great as the famous namesake of Constantine XI, noting that just as the first Constantine's mother was named Helena, so was the last emperor's mother. The parallel is used to frame Constantine XI as a worthy successor to the founder of the Christian Roman Empire.

people.summary_synthesized: Roman emperor from 306 to 337, Constantine I refounded the ancient city of Byzantium as Constantinople, establishing it as a new Christian imperial capital and earning him recognition as the progenitor of what later historians would call the Byzantine Empire. Through a series of civil wars he fought his way to sole rule, presided over the Council of Nicaea to unify the church, and commissioned foundational monuments including the original Hagia Sophia and the Church of the Holy Apostles; his 31-year reign was the longest since Augustus. His death left a contested succession and an unfinished Persian war, yet his transformation of the empire into a Christian autocracy proved so consequential that later rulers, from Justinian to the last emperor bearing his name, were measured against his legacy.

people.name: Maxentius
people.summary: Son of Maximian and the other imperial son passed over by the Tetrarchy's succession. He seized Rome, declared it tax-free, and used massive well-placed bribes to defeat both Severus and Galerius. His popularity in Rome later collapsed due to his pursuit of other men's wives, his ambivalent stance toward Christians, and the brutal killing of thousands of protesters. He died at the Battle of the Milvian Bridge when the pontoon bridge he had built collapsed and he was swept into the Tiber.

summaries_by_episode.3: Son of Maximian and the other imperial son passed over by the Tetrarchy's succession. He seized Rome, declared it tax-free, and used massive well-placed bribes to defeat both Severus and Galerius. His popularity in Rome later collapsed due to his pursuit of other men's wives, his ambivalent stance toward Christians, and the brutal killing of thousands of protesters. He died at the Battle of the Milvian Bridge when the pontoon bridge he had built collapsed and he was swept into the Tiber.

people.name: Fausta
people.summary: Daughter of Maximian and sister of Maxentius, given in marriage to Constantine as part of Maxentius's alliance-building with his fellow emperor of shaky legitimacy. Brownworth closes the episode by noting the next lecture will address whether a man who could kill his own wife and son can be seen as a protector of Christianity.

summaries_by_episode.3: Daughter of Maximian and sister of Maxentius, given in marriage to Constantine as part of Maxentius's alliance-building with his fellow emperor of shaky legitimacy. Brownworth closes the episode by noting the next lecture will address whether a man who could kill his own wife and son can be seen as a protector of Christianity.
summaries_by_episode.4: Constantine's wife who traveled with him on the ill-fated trip to Rome in early 326. She was killed by Constantine in her bath, amid swirling rumors of an affair or a plot against the emperor's life that were never entirely answered.

people.summary_synthesized: Daughter of Maximian and sister of Maxentius, Fausta was given in marriage to Constantine as part of an alliance between the two rulers. She later accompanied Constantine on his journey to Rome in early 326, after which she was killed by his order in her bath. The precise reason for her death — whether an alleged affair or a plot against Constantine — was never definitively established.

people.name: Constantia
people.summary: Mentioned as an example of Constantine's lack of imagination in naming his children — his daughter was named Constantia while his sons were all named variations of Constantine.

summaries_by_episode.3: Constantine's sister, given in marriage to Licinius to seal the new friendship between the two emperors after their meeting following the Battle of the Milvian Bridge.
summaries_by_episode.5: Mentioned as an example of Constantine's lack of imagination in naming his children — his daughter was named Constantia while his sons were all named variations of Constantine.

people.summary_synthesized: Sister of Constantine I, Constantia was given in marriage to the emperor Licinius to seal the alliance between the two rulers following the Battle of the Milvian Bridge. Her name also reflects the broader pattern in Constantine's family, where daughters received feminized forms of his own name while sons were given variations of it.

people.name: Athanasius of Alexandria
people.summary: A firebrand bishop elected by the Egyptians who flatly refused to reinstate Arius when Constantine pressured him to do so. He fled to Constantinople when it became clear only pro-Arian bishops would attend a new council, was deposed and banished to Trier in his absence, and his exile ultimately outlasted Constantine himself.

summaries_by_episode.4: A firebrand bishop elected by the Egyptians who flatly refused to reinstate Arius when Constantine pressured him to do so. He fled to Constantinople when it became clear only pro-Arian bishops would attend a new council, was deposed and banished to Trier in his absence, and his exile ultimately outlasted Constantine himself.

people.name: Crispus
people.summary: Constantine's eldest son, described as a brilliant general popular with both the army and the people — it was said that for Constantine's hundreds, Crispus had his thousands. He was arrested at Sirmium and executed on orders from his father for reasons never made entirely clear.

summaries_by_episode.3: Constantine's eldest son, described by Brownworth as energetic, brilliant, and even more well-liked than his father. He is mentioned as part of the bright future that seemed to await Constantine at the end of the civil wars, though the next episode will address his killing.
summaries_by_episode.4: Constantine's eldest son, described as a brilliant general popular with both the army and the people — it was said that for Constantine's hundreds, Crispus had his thousands. He was arrested at Sirmium and executed on orders from his father for reasons never made entirely clear.

people.summary_synthesized: Constantine's eldest son, Crispus was a brilliant general who enjoyed widespread popularity with both the army and the people, reportedly surpassing even his father in public affection. He was arrested at Sirmium and executed on his father's orders for reasons that were never made entirely clear.

people.name: Julius Constantius
people.summary: Constantine's half-brother and Julian's father. Constantius II accused him of poisoning Constantine, and he was pursued to his house and butchered along with his eldest son in the army's purge of imperial relatives.

summaries_by_episode.5: Constantine's half-brother and Julian's father. Constantius II accused him of poisoning Constantine, and he was pursued to his house and butchered along with his eldest son in the army's purge of imperial relatives.

people.name: Magnentius
people.summary: A usurper who donned the purple while Constans was away hunting, triggering the flight and capture of Constans. He was defeated the following year by Constantius II.

summaries_by_episode.5: A usurper who donned the purple while Constans was away hunting, triggering the flight and capture of Constans. He was defeated the following year by Constantius II.

people.name: Severus
people.summary: Galerius's chosen Caesar for the West, installed over Constantine's claim. He was sent to crush Maxentius in Italy but disaster struck when his army melted away at the very walls of Rome, bribed by Maxentius. He fled to Ravenna, was captured, abdicated, and killed.

summaries_by_episode.3: Galerius's chosen Caesar for the West, installed over Constantine's claim. He was sent to crush Maxentius in Italy but disaster struck when his army melted away at the very walls of Rome, bribed by Maxentius. He fled to Ravenna, was captured, abdicated, and killed.

people.role: Roman Emperor (Caesar, then Augustus of the West)

people.name: Shapur II
people.summary: The Persian king who sent Constantius a florid ultimatum demanding Mesopotamia and Armenia, then followed through with raids on Roman strongholds. When Julian invaded, Shapur avoided direct engagement, relying on harassment attacks, and his approaching main army helped force Julian's retreat from Ctesiphon.

summaries_by_episode.5: The Persian king who sent Constantius a florid ultimatum demanding Mesopotamia and Armenia, then followed through with raids on Roman strongholds. When Julian invaded, Shapur avoided direct engagement, relying on harassment attacks, and his approaching main army helped force Julian's retreat from Ctesiphon.

people.name: Libanius
people.summary: A celebrated philosopher who had rejected Christianity and remained unabashedly pagan. Julian first heard him while wandering the Greek world, and his Christian teachers, worried by Julian's interest, forbade him from attending the lectures — so Julian had them copied down and read to him instead.

summaries_by_episode.5: A celebrated philosopher who had rejected Christianity and remained unabashedly pagan. Julian first heard him while wandering the Greek world, and his Christian teachers, worried by Julian's interest, forbade him from attending the lectures — so Julian had them copied down and read to him instead.

people.name: Constantine II
people.summary: The eldest son of Constantine who tried to lord it over his brothers. He invaded Italy to confront his youngest brother Constans, but underestimated him, was ambushed, and killed.

summaries_by_episode.5: The eldest son of Constantine who tried to lord it over his brothers. He invaded Italy to confront his youngest brother Constans, but underestimated him, was ambushed, and killed.

people.name: Valens
people.summary: The commander of Licinius's Danube forces, appointed as co-emperor in desperation after Constantine defeated Licinius's main army in 316. He managed to fight Constantine to a standstill in Thrace, but when Licinius came to terms with Constantine, Valens was executed as the price of the treaty — a fate Brownworth notes one 'can't help but sympathize with.'

summaries_by_episode.3: The commander of Licinius's Danube forces, appointed as co-emperor in desperation after Constantine defeated Licinius's main army in 316. He managed to fight Constantine to a standstill in Thrace, but when Licinius came to terms with Constantine, Valens was executed as the price of the treaty — a fate Brownworth notes one 'can't help but sympathize with.'

people.name: Constantius II
people.summary: The middle son of Constantine who consolidated power by spreading a rumor blaming his half-uncles for poisoning Constantine, triggering the family massacre. He proved a capable and determined commander, but died of illness in Cilicia while marching to confront Julian, naming him successor on his deathbed.

summaries_by_episode.5: The middle son of Constantine who consolidated power by spreading a rumor blaming his half-uncles for poisoning Constantine, triggering the family massacre. He proved a capable and determined commander, but died of illness in Cilicia while marching to confront Julian, naming him successor on his deathbed.

people.name: Constans I
people.summary: The youngest son of Constantine who defeated and killed his brother Constantine II when invaded. Once secure on his throne, he neglected the army in favor of his blonde female prisoners and hunting, leading to a revolt by the usurper Magnentius.

summaries_by_episode.5: The youngest son of Constantine who defeated and killed his brother Constantine II when invaded. Once secure on his throne, he neglected the army in favor of his blonde female prisoners and hunting, leading to a revolt by the usurper Magnentius.

people.name: Gallus Caesar
people.summary: Julian's older brother who was spared in the family massacre because of his young age. Transported from a kind of house-arrest to the throne, he proved unfit to reign, spending his time indulging a cruel streak until Constantius had him assassinated.

summaries_by_episode.5: Julian's older brother who was spared in the family massacre because of his young age. Transported from a kind of house-arrest to the throne, he proved unfit to reign, spending his time indulging a cruel streak until Constantius had him assassinated.

people.name: Saint Basil of Caesarea
people.summary: A 4th-century Byzantine theologian cited by Brownworth to illustrate the Byzantine theological objection to holy war: Basil preached that killing, though sometimes justified, could never be praiseworthy, let alone grounds for the remission of sin. This teaching exemplified why Byzantines were scandalized by crusading ideology.

summaries_by_episode.15: A 4th-century Byzantine theologian cited by Brownworth to illustrate the Byzantine theological objection to holy war: Basil preached that killing, though sometimes justified, could never be praiseworthy, let alone grounds for the remission of sin. This teaching exemplified why Byzantines were scandalized by crusading ideology.

people.name: Jovian
people.summary: The deeply uninspiring Christian soldier elected by the army to replace Julian after his death. His election signaled a final rejection of Julian's pagan policies.

summaries_by_episode.5: The deeply uninspiring Christian soldier elected by the army to replace Julian after his death. His election signaled a final rejection of Julian's pagan policies.

people.name: Julian
people.summary: A complex, enigmatic philosopher-emperor who survived a childhood massacre of his family to become Caesar at 23. He secretly abandoned Christianity for paganism and devoted his reign to reversing Constantine's religious legacy, but died in battle against the Persians before his ambitions could be realized.

summaries_by_episode.4: Mentioned briefly at the end of the episode as the subject of the next lecture — described as experiencing an unlikely rise and tragic fall, and as the last of the pagan emperors.
summaries_by_episode.5: A complex, enigmatic philosopher-emperor who survived a childhood massacre of his family to become Caesar at 23. He secretly abandoned Christianity for paganism and devoted his reign to reversing Constantine's religious legacy, but died in battle against the Persians before his ambitions could be realized.
summaries_by_episode.6: Described as a misguided visionary who squandered tremendous talent in a fruitless attempt to restore paganism and destroy Christianity. He was the last emperor to rule a united empire and the last male relative of Constantine the Great, leaving the empire in dynastic exhaustion.

people.summary_synthesized: Julian was a philosopher-emperor who survived a childhood massacre of his family to become Caesar at 23 and later sole ruler of the Roman Empire, the last male relative of Constantine the Great. Having secretly abandoned Christianity, he devoted his reign to reversing Constantine's religious legacy and restoring paganism, but died in battle against the Persians before those ambitions could be realized. He was the last pagan emperor and the last to rule a united empire, leaving the dynasty in exhaustion at his death.

people.name: Augustine of Hippo
people.summary: Augustine is mentioned only by association with the city of Hippo, which Brownworth calls 'the city of Augustine' when describing Belisarius's entry into the city after defeating the Vandal army.

summaries_by_episode.8: Augustine is mentioned only by association with the city of Hippo, which Brownworth calls 'the city of Augustine' when describing Belisarius's entry into the city after defeating the Vandal army.

people.role: Christian theologian and bishop of Hippo

people.name: Aetius
people.summary: A barbarian Germanic general who had spent 30 years as the power behind the Western throne before being rashly killed by Emperor Valentinian III with his own hands. His murder destabilized the West and triggered a chain of events leading to further chaos.

summaries_by_episode.6: A barbarian Germanic general who had spent 30 years as the power behind the Western throne before being rashly killed by Emperor Valentinian III with his own hands. His murder destabilized the West and triggered a chain of events leading to further chaos.

people.role: Roman General, de facto ruler of the Western Empire

people.name: Petronius Maximus
people.summary: Described only as an elderly senator of around 60 who replaced Valentinian III, quickly forced the widowed empress to marry him, bribed everyone for control of the palace, then showed remarkable lack of tact by embracing Valentinian's murderers. When the Vandals arrived, he advised citizens to flee, was blamed for the disaster, dismembered by the populace, and his pieces thrown into the Tiber.

summaries_by_episode.6: Described only as an elderly senator of around 60 who replaced Valentinian III, quickly forced the widowed empress to marry him, bribed everyone for control of the palace, then showed remarkable lack of tact by embracing Valentinian's murderers. When the Vandals arrived, he advised citizens to flee, was blamed for the disaster, dismembered by the populace, and his pieces thrown into the Tiber.

people.name: Aspar
people.summary: An Arian barbarian general who controlled the Eastern Empire from behind the throne for at least 23 years. He handpicked Leo I as emperor expecting another docile puppet, but Leo systematically undermined him by recruiting the Isaurians. Aspar attempted to assassinate Zeno and infiltrate his troops before Leo finally summoned him and his sons to the palace and had them killed.

summaries_by_episode.6: An Arian barbarian general who controlled the Eastern Empire from behind the throne for at least 23 years. He handpicked Leo I as emperor expecting another docile puppet, but Leo systematically undermined him by recruiting the Isaurians. Aspar attempted to assassinate Zeno and infiltrate his troops before Leo finally summoned him and his sons to the palace and had them killed.

people.role: Barbarian General and Power Broker of the Eastern Empire

people.name: Pope Leo I
people.summary: The pope who famously turned away Attila the Hun three years before the Vandal sack of Rome, and then went out to negotiate with the Vandal invaders. This time he could only secure a promise that no citizens would be killed and no buildings destroyed — a promise the Vandals largely kept.

summaries_by_episode.6: The pope who famously turned away Attila the Hun three years before the Vandal sack of Rome, and then went out to negotiate with the Vandal invaders. This time he could only secure a promise that no citizens would be killed and no buildings destroyed — a promise the Vandals largely kept.

people.name: Leo I
people.summary: A 59-year-old member of Aspar's staff chosen as a compliant puppet emperor, Leo proved to be anything but. Though uneducated, he possessed abundant common sense and worked systematically to break free from Germanic domination of his court, ultimately having Aspar and his sons killed. He was the first emperor crowned with a ceremony involving the Christian patriarch, a tradition that continued until the empire's end.

summaries_by_episode.6: A 59-year-old member of Aspar's staff chosen as a compliant puppet emperor, Leo proved to be anything but. Though uneducated, he possessed abundant common sense and worked systematically to break free from Germanic domination of his court, ultimately having Aspar and his sons killed. He was the first emperor crowned with a ceremony involving the Christian patriarch, a tradition that continued until the empire's end.

people.name: Theodosius II
people.summary: Built the second Hagia Sophia eleven years after the first was destroyed in a riot, described by Brownworth as a "rather uninspired replica" that nonetheless became the main church of the capital because of its location next to the great palace.

summaries_by_episode.9: Built the second Hagia Sophia eleven years after the first was destroyed in a riot, described by Brownworth as a "rather uninspired replica" that nonetheless became the main church of the capital because of its location next to the great palace.

people.name: Attila the Hun
people.summary: Brownworth invokes Attila the Hun as the supreme test of Constantinople's land walls — even he, the Scourge of God himself, had turned away when he saw them. This historical benchmark is used to underscore just how formidable the Theodosian Walls were before the Turkish cannon reduced them.

summaries_by_episode.5: Mentioned in Brownworth's epilogue as the looming threat that made the Eastern Empire's survival seem in doubt after Julian's death and the subsequent weakening of the empire.
summaries_by_episode.6: Three years before the Vandal sack of Rome, Attila the Hun had stood at the gates of the city but was successfully turned away by Pope Leo I. He is also mentioned as the former employer of Orestes, the father of Romulus Augustulus.
summaries_by_episode.16: Brownworth invokes Attila the Hun as the supreme test of Constantinople's land walls — even he, the Scourge of God himself, had turned away when he saw them. This historical benchmark is used to underscore just how formidable the Theodosian Walls were before the Turkish cannon reduced them.

people.summary_synthesized: Attila the Hun, ruler of the Hunnic Empire in the mid-fifth century, posed an existential threat to both halves of the Roman world, advancing to the gates of Rome itself before being turned away by Pope Leo I. His failure to breach Constantinople's Theodosian Walls became a lasting measure of their impregnability, as even he — widely called the Scourge of God — withdrew rather than assault them. Beyond his military campaigns, his influence extended into Roman politics through former subordinates such as Orestes, whose son Romulus Augustulus became the last Western Roman emperor.

people.name: Valentinian III
people.summary: The puppet Western emperor who decided he wanted to rule in his own right and rashly killed Aetius with his own hands, only to be assassinated himself a few months later by Aetius's disgruntled officers.

summaries_by_episode.6: The puppet Western emperor who decided he wanted to rule in his own right and rashly killed Aetius with his own hands, only to be assassinated himself a few months later by Aetius's disgruntled officers.

people.name: Zeno
people.summary: A barbarian chieftain from the Taurus Mountains of Armenia, Zeno rose to power by marrying Leo I's daughter and eventually became sole emperor of the East. His reign was the most eventful in Byzantine history, seeing the fall of the Western Empire and the birth of the medieval papacy, though he was chronically unpopular due to his foreign origins and faced nearly constant rebellion.

summaries_by_episode.5: Briefly introduced as the subject of the next episode — the man who broke free from his barbarian generals, whose unlikely combination of cleverness and force of will saved an empire that should have fallen.
summaries_by_episode.6: A barbarian chieftain from the Taurus Mountains of Armenia, Zeno rose to power by marrying Leo I's daughter and eventually became sole emperor of the East. His reign was the most eventful in Byzantine history, seeing the fall of the Western Empire and the birth of the medieval papacy, though he was chronically unpopular due to his foreign origins and faced nearly constant rebellion.
summaries_by_episode.7: The emperor whose remarkable reign immediately preceded the period Brownworth covers in this episode. Despite being deeply unpopular and even having to flee his own capital in disguise at one point, Zeno managed to survive and left the throne far stronger than he found it — a stunning achievement that set the stage for Justinian's later glory.

people.summary_synthesized: A chieftain from the Taurus Mountains of Armenia, Zeno rose to prominence by marrying into the imperial family and eventually became sole emperor of the Eastern Roman Empire. His reign was among the most turbulent and eventful in Byzantine history, spanning the fall of the Western Empire and the emergence of the medieval papacy, while near-constant rebellion over his foreign origins twice drove him from power. Through a combination of cleverness and tenacity he survived every challenge, leaving the throne considerably stronger than he had found it and laying the groundwork for the later achievements of Justinian I.

people.name: Anastasius I
people.summary: The emperor under whom Justin I served as commander of the palace guard. After Anastasius died, the succession dispute gave Justin his opening to seize the throne. Brownworth notes that Anastasius, like Justin, tended toward frugality and left the imperial treasury full — a fiscal conservatism that contrasted sharply with Justinian's lavish spending.

summaries_by_episode.7: The emperor under whom Justin I served as commander of the palace guard. After Anastasius died, the succession dispute gave Justin his opening to seize the throne. Brownworth notes that Anastasius, like Justin, tended toward frugality and left the imperial treasury full — a fiscal conservatism that contrasted sharply with Justinian's lavish spending.

people.name: Odoacer
people.summary: The barbarian leader of three tribes who demanded one-third of Italy, killed Orestes when refused, and deposed Romulus Augustulus, ending the Western Empire. He chose to give lip service to Zeno's eastern authority rather than installing a new puppet emperor in Ravenna, sending Zeno the imperial insignia and asking only for the title of patrician. He was ultimately tricked into a joint rule by Theodoric and murdered at a banquet.

summaries_by_episode.6: The barbarian leader of three tribes who demanded one-third of Italy, killed Orestes when refused, and deposed Romulus Augustulus, ending the Western Empire. He chose to give lip service to Zeno's eastern authority rather than installing a new puppet emperor in Ravenna, sending Zeno the imperial insignia and asking only for the title of patrician. He was ultimately tricked into a joint rule by Theodoric and murdered at a banquet.

people.name: Justin I
people.summary: A rags-to-riches emperor who came to Constantinople with nothing and rose through the army to command the palace guard under Anastasius I. He secured his election as emperor through a combination of military positioning and liberal bribing of soldiers. Nearly 70 at accession, he lacked practical governing knowledge but was wise enough to surround himself with talented advisors — most crucially his nephew Justinian, whom he educated and ultimately adopted.

summaries_by_episode.7: A rags-to-riches emperor who came to Constantinople with nothing and rose through the army to command the palace guard under Anastasius I. He secured his election as emperor through a combination of military positioning and liberal bribing of soldiers. Nearly 70 at accession, he lacked practical governing knowledge but was wise enough to surround himself with talented advisors — most crucially his nephew Justinian, whom he educated and ultimately adopted.

people.name: Theodoric the Great
people.summary: The brilliant young leader of the Ostrogoths, recruited by Zeno to suppress the rebel Illus and then sent into Italy to displace Odoacer, taking his entire people with him. He defeated Illus by besieging his Isaurian fortress for four years, and subdued Odoacer by blockading him in Ravenna, feigning a joint rule, and then personally striking him down at a banquet. Theodoric ruled Italy wisely for 33 years, giving lip service to Byzantine authority.

summaries_by_episode.6: The brilliant young leader of the Ostrogoths, recruited by Zeno to suppress the rebel Illus and then sent into Italy to displace Odoacer, taking his entire people with him. He defeated Illus by besieging his Isaurian fortress for four years, and subdued Odoacer by blockading him in Ravenna, feigning a joint rule, and then personally striking him down at a banquet. Theodoric ruled Italy wisely for 33 years, giving lip service to Byzantine authority.

people.role: King of the Ostrogoths, King of Italy

people.name: Romulus Augustulus
people.summary: Son of the general Orestes, placed on the western throne as a child with two famous namesakes to live up to. Odoacer felt sorry for the pathetic young emperor and rather than killing him, sent him into comfortable exile in Campania. Though his death date is unrecorded, his abdication in 476 marked the end of the Roman Empire in the West.

summaries_by_episode.6: Son of the general Orestes, placed on the western throne as a child with two famous namesakes to live up to. Odoacer felt sorry for the pathetic young emperor and rather than killing him, sent him into comfortable exile in Campania. Though his death date is unrecorded, his abdication in 476 marked the end of the Roman Empire in the West.
summaries_by_episode.17: Brownworth laments that Western history teaches the last Roman Emperor was 'a sad little boy named Romulus Augustulus,' ignoring the millennium-long continuation of Rome in Byzantium. He contrasts this with the heroic Constantine XI to illustrate how Byzantine history has been marginalized.

people.summary_synthesized: The last emperor of the Western Roman Empire, Romulus Augustulus was a child placed on the throne by his father, the general Orestes, and reigned only briefly before being deposed by Odoacer in 476. Rather than executing him, Odoacer exiled the young emperor to Campania, where he lived out his days in comfort; his death date was never recorded. His abdication in 476 is conventionally treated as the end of the Roman Empire, though the empire continued in the East for nearly another millennium.

people.name: Leo II
people.summary: Zeno's seven-year-old son who was nominated as successor by Leo I. As his first official act, the child appointed his father Zeno as co-emperor, and died nine months later, making Zeno sole ruler of the Eastern Empire.

summaries_by_episode.6: Zeno's seven-year-old son who was nominated as successor by Leo I. As his first official act, the child appointed his father Zeno as co-emperor, and died nine months later, making Zeno sole ruler of the Eastern Empire.

people.name: Julius Nepos
people.summary: The western emperor at the start of Zeno's reign who was overthrown by the general Orestes. When Odoacer sent Zeno the imperial insignia after deposing Romulus Augustulus, Zeno cleverly deflected the issue by telling Odoacer to address his request to the still-living exiled western emperor Julius Nepos.

summaries_by_episode.6: The western emperor at the start of Zeno's reign who was overthrown by the general Orestes. When Odoacer sent Zeno the imperial insignia after deposing Romulus Augustulus, Zeno cleverly deflected the issue by telling Odoacer to address his request to the still-living exiled western emperor Julius Nepos.

people.name: Basiliscus
people.summary: The emperor's brother-in-law, described as a well-educated but incompetent fool with a burning desire for power. After commanding the disastrous African naval campaign against the Vandals, he later usurped the throne while Zeno fled to Isauria, but his reign was a catalogue of mismanagement — alienating the army, squandering support, appointing Timothy the Weasel as his advisor, and triggering a massive fire and religious riots — until Zeno returned and exiled him to Cappadocia to freeze to death.

summaries_by_episode.6: The emperor's brother-in-law, described as a well-educated but incompetent fool with a burning desire for power. After commanding the disastrous African naval campaign against the Vandals, he later usurped the throne while Zeno fled to Isauria, but his reign was a catalogue of mismanagement — alienating the army, squandering support, appointing Timothy the Weasel as his advisor, and triggering a massive fire and religious riots — until Zeno returned and exiled him to Cappadocia to freeze to death.
summaries_by_episode.8: Basiliscus is mentioned as the 'dismal' commander of the last Byzantine army that had invaded North Africa, 100,000 strong, who managed within days to turn what should have been an overwhelming victory into an unmitigated disaster. His failure set the bar against which Belisarius's much smaller but more skillful campaign is contrasted.

people.summary_synthesized: A brother-in-law of the emperor Zeno, Basiliscus commanded the catastrophic Byzantine naval expedition against the Vandals in North Africa, squandering a force of roughly 100,000 men and turning near-certain victory into total defeat. He subsequently usurped the throne during Zeno's exile to Isauria, but his brief reign was marked by mismanagement, the alienation of the army, poor counsel, religious riots, and a devastating fire. Zeno eventually returned to reclaim power, and Basiliscus was exiled to Cappadocia, where he died of exposure.

people.name: Narses
people.summary: Narses is described as a eunuch in his 60s whose condition prevented him from wanting the throne himself, and the most powerful figure at court. Theodora and Justinian sent him to Italy with vague orders to obey Belisarius 'so far as seems consistent with the public good,' effectively to watch and limit the general. He undermined Belisarius's command, backed insubordinate officers, and his presence split the army.

summaries_by_episode.8: Narses is described as a eunuch in his 60s whose condition prevented him from wanting the throne himself, and the most powerful figure at court. Theodora and Justinian sent him to Italy with vague orders to obey Belisarius 'so far as seems consistent with the public good,' effectively to watch and limit the general. He undermined Belisarius's command, backed insubordinate officers, and his presence split the army.
summaries_by_episode.9: Described as the most powerful member of Justinian's court and well into his seventies when appointed, Narses was given what Belisarius never had: full powers and an army of 35,000 plus back pay for the troops. Despite his only prior military experience being the suppression of the Nika Riots and the losing of Milan, he proved a superb organizer and decisively defeated and killed Totila, ending Gothic resistance.
summaries_by_episode.10: The eunuch who completed Justinian's reconquest of Italy, but was recalled and humiliated by the Empress Sophia. According to legend, he muttered that he would tie her a knot she would not undo in her lifetime, and then invited the Lombards into Italy, starting a long bitter war that cost the empire most of its Italian territory.

people.role: Byzantine eunuch courtier and military commander
people.summary_synthesized: A eunuch courtier who rose to become the most powerful figure at Justinian's court, Narses first appeared in Italy as a check on Belisarius, where his divided loyalties undermined the general's command. Appointed supreme commander in his seventies with full powers and a well-supplied army of 35,000, he proved a capable organizer despite limited prior experience, decisively defeating and killing the Ostrogothic king Totila and completing the reconquest of Italy. He was later recalled and humiliated by the Empress Sophia, and according to legend retaliated by inviting the Lombards into Italy, triggering a prolonged war that cost the empire most of the territory he had won.

people.name: Justinian I
people.summary: Born Flavius Petrus Sabatius in modern-day Karasengrad around 483, he was adopted and educated by his uncle Justin I and took the name Justinian. He rose to effective control of government well before being formally crowned, and his tireless ambition — earning him the nickname 'the emperor who never sleeps' — drove a spectacular explosion of conquest, law, and culture that Brownworth calls the Byzantine Renaissance. Brownworth considers him his favorite Byzantine emperor, noting that Justinian dreamed larger than other men and sought to change the world rather than merely live in it.

summaries_by_episode.4: Mentioned in passing as the emperor who completely demolished and rebuilt the Church of the Holy Apostles, which had been Constantine's mausoleum. The rebuilt church eventually also disappeared, leaving no trace of Constantine's magnificent tomb.
summaries_by_episode.6: Mentioned only in prospect as the subject of the next episode, described as the greatest of all Byzantine emperors and the last man to dream on a truly imperial scale, who 36 years after Zeno's death would rise to restore the empire's glory and attempt to reclaim the West.
summaries_by_episode.7: Born Flavius Petrus Sabatius in modern-day Karasengrad around 483, he was adopted and educated by his uncle Justin I and took the name Justinian. He rose to effective control of government well before being formally crowned, and his tireless ambition — earning him the nickname 'the emperor who never sleeps' — drove a spectacular explosion of conquest, law, and culture that Brownworth calls the Byzantine Renaissance. Brownworth considers him his favorite Byzantine emperor, noting that Justinian dreamed larger than other men and sought to change the world rather than merely live in it.
summaries_by_episode.8: Justinian is portrayed as a visionary emperor whose greatest dream was the reconquest of the Western Empire. Brownworth depicts him as dependent on Belisarius to fulfill this vision, but also susceptible to Theodora's jealousies and capable of panic — recalling Belisarius at critical moments — yet ultimately recovering from the plague and asserting his rule.
summaries_by_episode.9: The dominant figure of the episode, Justinian is portrayed as enormously ambitious — reconquering Africa, Italy, and a slice of Spain — but also fatally cautious toward capable subordinates, especially Belisarius. In his final years he shifted from military ambition to theological dispute and monumental building, most famously the Hagia Sophia, and Brownworth frames him as the last great ruler of the old Roman world whose conquests could not outlast him.
summaries_by_episode.10: Referenced as the benchmark emperor whose 38-year reign saw momentous reconquests of Africa, Spain, and Italy, but whose death left the empire overextended. Brownworth also identifies him as the last Roman emperor, with Heraclius being the first Greek one, and notes that a religious controversy over Christ's nature had divided the empire since Justinian's day.
summaries_by_episode.12: Invoked repeatedly as the benchmark against whom Basil I measured himself. Basil saw himself as a new Justinian, determined to reconquer western territories, revise the law code, and undertake massive building projects — including repairing Justinian's great church the Hagia Sophia and rebuilding the Church of the Holy Apostles that Justinian had restored.
summaries_by_episode.17: Brownworth calls Justinian Byzantium's greatest emperor, crediting him with giving Western civilization Roman law and state organization. Justinian's famous exclamation 'Solomon, I have surpassed you' upon completing the Hagia Sophia is cited as the crowning expression of Byzantine achievement.

people.summary_synthesized: Justinian I, born Flavius Petrus Sabatius around 483 and adopted by his uncle Justin I, ruled the Byzantine Empire for nearly four decades and pursued an ambitious reconquest of the western Mediterranean, recovering Africa, Italy, and a portion of Spain. His reign also produced a comprehensive codification of Roman law and a transformation of the built world, most famously the Hagia Sophia—whose completion prompted his reported exclamation, "Solomon, I have surpassed you." Though his conquests proved difficult to sustain beyond his death, his legal and cultural legacy shaped Western civilization, and later emperors such as Basil I measured their own ambitions explicitly against his example.

people.name: Amalasuntha
people.summary: Amalasuntha was an extraordinarily educated woman who ruled the Ostrogothic kingdom as regent for her young son, but was resented by the nobility who wrested her son away to give him a military upbringing. After her son died at 16, she hatched a plot with Justinian to flee and retake Italy with Byzantine help, but her cousin Theodahad seized power, offered her co-rule, then had her strangled in her bath.

summaries_by_episode.8: Amalasuntha was an extraordinarily educated woman who ruled the Ostrogothic kingdom as regent for her young son, but was resented by the nobility who wrested her son away to give him a military upbringing. After her son died at 16, she hatched a plot with Justinian to flee and retake Italy with Byzantine help, but her cousin Theodahad seized power, offered her co-rule, then had her strangled in her bath.

people.name: Theodora
people.summary: A young actress whom Justinian met through his blue-faction informer Macedonia, Theodora was beautiful, beguiling, and an ardent supporter of the Blues. She and Justinian accepted each other as intellectual equals from the start, and her prodigious energy matched his own. Her most celebrated moment in this episode is the speech she delivered during the Nika Revolt, shaming Justinian's terrified advisors into staying and fighting rather than fleeing — declaring that 'the imperial purple makes the best shroud.'

summaries_by_episode.7: A young actress whom Justinian met through his blue-faction informer Macedonia, Theodora was beautiful, beguiling, and an ardent supporter of the Blues. She and Justinian accepted each other as intellectual equals from the start, and her prodigious energy matched his own. Her most celebrated moment in this episode is the speech she delivered during the Nika Revolt, shaming Justinian's terrified advisors into staying and fighting rather than fleeing — declaring that 'the imperial purple makes the best shroud.'
summaries_by_episode.8: Theodora is depicted as jealously guarding Justinian's reputation and deeply suspicious of Belisarius, fearing he was too brilliant and popular — the stuff of which emperors are made. When Justinian nearly died of plague, she maneuvered to retain power, and upon his recovery punished those who had dared discuss succession without her consent.
summaries_by_episode.9: Theodora's jealousy of Belisarius led her to disgrace him and fragment the Italian command among five generals, directly enabling Totila's resurgence. Brownworth notes that while she was initially a faithful partner to Justinian, her later intrigues — including working against Belisarius through her friendship with Antonina — sabotaged the Italian campaign and his religious policy; her death from likely cancer in 548 paradoxically freed Justinian to restore Belisarius to favor.

people.role: Byzantine Empress, wife of Justinian I
people.summary_synthesized: Theodora rose from actress to empress as the intellectual and political equal of Justinian, whom she is credited with saving during the Nika Revolt of 532 by delivering a speech that shamed his advisors into fighting rather than fleeing. In the later years of her reign she wielded power jealously, maneuvering to control succession when Justinian fell ill with plague and working to undermine Belisarius, whose popularity she viewed as a threat. Her death in 548 paradoxically benefited the Italian campaign she had helped sabotage, as Justinian was then free to restore Belisarius to command.

people.name: Belisarius
people.summary: Only in his twenties during the Nika Revolt, Belisarius is described by Brownworth as already perhaps the most brilliant tactician in Roman history. He devised the plan to suppress the revolt and led the assault on the Hippodrome alongside Mundus and Narcissus. Brownworth teases his fuller story for the next episode, calling him the empire's most brilliant and tragic general who would fulfill Justinian's dream of epic reconquests.

summaries_by_episode.7: Only in his twenties during the Nika Revolt, Belisarius is described by Brownworth as already perhaps the most brilliant tactician in Roman history. He devised the plan to suppress the revolt and led the assault on the Hippodrome alongside Mundus and Narcissus. Brownworth teases his fuller story for the next episode, calling him the empire's most brilliant and tragic general who would fulfill Justinian's dream of epic reconquests.
summaries_by_episode.8: Belisarius is the central figure of this episode — a Romanized Greek born in modern-day Bulgaria whom Gibbon called 'the Scipio of New Rome.' Brownworth presents him as a natural leader of extraordinary brilliance and loyalty who reconquered North Africa and most of Italy, yet was repeatedly thwarted by Theodora's jealousy and Justinian's distrust, spending much of his later career in disappointing frustration.
summaries_by_episode.9: The great general who conducted Justinian's second Italian campaign with only 4,000 men and no supreme authority, achieving a stubborn stalemate against Totila despite impossible odds. Brownworth portrays him as loyal to a fault, repeatedly undermined by jealousy at court, yet never embittered; he ended his career defeating a Hunnic raid in his mid-fifties with improvised volunteers, and died five years later at age sixty.
summaries_by_episode.13: Mentioned as a historical benchmark — Brownworth calls Nikephoros Phokas possibly the greatest general since Belisarius, and also references Belisarius' earlier reconquest of Sicily in the 6th century.

people.summary_synthesized: A Romanized Greek born in modern-day Bulgaria, Belisarius was the preeminent Byzantine general of the sixth century, reconquering North Africa, Sicily, and much of Italy on behalf of Justinian I. Despite these achievements, his career was repeatedly undermined by Theodora's jealousy and Justinian's distrust, and his second Italian campaign was conducted with only 4,000 men and no supreme authority, producing a stubborn stalemate against Totila rather than the decisive victory his resources might have permitted. He remained loyal throughout and never grew embittered, ending his active service by organizing improvised volunteers to repel a Hunnic raid in his mid-fifties before his death at around sixty.

people.name: Procopius
people.summary: Procopius was Belisarius's personal secretary and is described by Brownworth as 'our primary historian for the period.' He appears in the narrative when, while stopped in Sicily, he happened to run into an old friend whose slave had returned from Carthage, providing Belisarius with crucial intelligence about Vandal unpreparedness.

summaries_by_episode.8: Procopius was Belisarius's personal secretary and is described by Brownworth as 'our primary historian for the period.' He appears in the narrative when, while stopped in Sicily, he happened to run into an old friend whose slave had returned from Carthage, providing Belisarius with crucial intelligence about Vandal unpreparedness.
summaries_by_episode.9: Cited by Brownworth as the source for the detail that only 500 citizens remained in Rome when Totila took possession of it, describing the city as an almost deserted capital.

people.role: Byzantine historian, personal secretary to Belisarius
people.summary_synthesized: A secretary to the general Belisarius and the primary historical source for the mid-sixth century Byzantine period, Procopius documented key moments of Justinian's campaigns in the west. His account records the intelligence gathered before the African expedition against the Vandals, as well as the desolation of Rome under the Ostrogothic king Totila, whom he credits with leaving the city so depopulated that only around 500 inhabitants remained.

people.name: Khosrow I
people.summary: Brownworth calls Khosrow 'undoubtedly the greatest of all the Persian kings, and a worthy match for Justinian.' He reformed Persia's government, created its first standing army, founded a medical academy, codified Zoroastrian scriptures, and even introduced chess from China. Despite accepting 5,000 pounds of gold for peace, he invaded again, sacked Antioch, and killed or enslaved its entire population.

summaries_by_episode.8: Brownworth calls Khosrow 'undoubtedly the greatest of all the Persian kings, and a worthy match for Justinian.' He reformed Persia's government, created its first standing army, founded a medical academy, codified Zoroastrian scriptures, and even introduced chess from China. Despite accepting 5,000 pounds of gold for peace, he invaded again, sacked Antioch, and killed or enslaved its entire population.

people.name: Euphemia
people.summary: Wife of Justin I, born a slave and formerly a concubine before Justin bought and married her. Despite her own humble origins, she fiercely guarded the dignity of her imperial station and was bitterly opposed to Justinian's plan to marry the actress Theodora. Her opposition blocked the marriage until her death in 523, after which Justinian quickly arranged repeal of the law forbidding patricians to marry below their station.

summaries_by_episode.7: Wife of Justin I, born a slave and formerly a concubine before Justin bought and married her. Despite her own humble origins, she fiercely guarded the dignity of her imperial station and was bitterly opposed to Justinian's plan to marry the actress Theodora. Her opposition blocked the marriage until her death in 523, after which Justinian quickly arranged repeal of the law forbidding patricians to marry below their station.

people.role: Empress consort, wife of Justin I

people.name: Justin II
people.summary: Justinian's nephew who became emperor the morning after Justinian's death, described as the first emperor to be crowned in the Hagia Sophia and acclaimed by the people. His accession marks the end of the age Brownworth frames as the last of the old Roman world.

summaries_by_episode.9: Justinian's nephew who became emperor the morning after Justinian's death, described as the first emperor to be crowned in the Hagia Sophia and acclaimed by the people. His accession marks the end of the age Brownworth frames as the last of the old Roman world.

people.role: Byzantine Emperor, nephew of Justinian I

people.name: Gelimer
people.summary: Gelimer seized the Vandal throne by killing his cousin, the previous king, dismissing Justinian's protest with a curt letter saying 'nothing is more desirable than that a monarch should mind his own business.' He twice froze in grief upon finding his brother dead on the battlefield, allowing Belisarius to rout his forces; he finally surrendered after hiding in the mountains and at the triumph prostrated himself before Justinian whispering 'Vanity of vanities, all is vanity.'

summaries_by_episode.8: Gelimer seized the Vandal throne by killing his cousin, the previous king, dismissing Justinian's protest with a curt letter saying 'nothing is more desirable than that a monarch should mind his own business.' He twice froze in grief upon finding his brother dead on the battlefield, allowing Belisarius to rout his forces; he finally surrendered after hiding in the mountains and at the triumph prostrated himself before Justinian whispering 'Vanity of vanities, all is vanity.'

people.name: Theodahad
people.summary: Theodahad was Amalasuntha's cousin who seized power and offered her a joint rule, only to quickly regret it. He had her strangled in her bath, giving Justinian the pretext for a Byzantine invasion of Italy.

summaries_by_episode.8: Theodahad was Amalasuntha's cousin who seized power and offered her a joint rule, only to quickly regret it. He had her strangled in her bath, giving Justinian the pretext for a Byzantine invasion of Italy.

people.name: Vittigis
people.summary: Vittigis was the Gothic noble crowned king after his predecessor was executed for failing to defend Italy. He announced he would not defend Rome and withdrew to Ravenna, then besieged Rome for a year and nine days. He was eventually tricked by Belisarius into surrendering, having offered Belisarius the western imperial crown — an offer Belisarius used as a ruse to capture him.

summaries_by_episode.8: Vittigis was the Gothic noble crowned king after his predecessor was executed for failing to defend Italy. He announced he would not defend Rome and withdrew to Ravenna, then besieged Rome for a year and nine days. He was eventually tricked by Belisarius into surrendering, having offered Belisarius the western imperial crown — an offer Belisarius used as a ruse to capture him.

people.name: Totila
people.summary: Totila is presented as the antithesis of the corrupt Byzantine commanders — shrewd, compassionate, and in his mid-twenties when he rose to lead the Goths. He appealed to the poor and middle classes with promises of liberation, redistributed land, defeated a 12,000-strong imperial army, captured Rome, yet governed with remarkable mercy; he was killed at the decisive battle against Narses and his death ended the Gothic kingdom in Italy.

summaries_by_episode.9: Totila is presented as the antithesis of the corrupt Byzantine commanders — shrewd, compassionate, and in his mid-twenties when he rose to lead the Goths. He appealed to the poor and middle classes with promises of liberation, redistributed land, defeated a 12,000-strong imperial army, captured Rome, yet governed with remarkable mercy; he was killed at the decisive battle against Narses and his death ended the Gothic kingdom in Italy.

people.name: Phocas
people.summary: A 55-year-old non-commissioned officer placed on the throne by a mutinous army, Brownworth describes him as having been 'somewhat kindly described as a sadistic monster.' His insecurity on the throne led to a bloody reign of paranoid executions, military disasters against Persia, and the introduction of mutilation and torture into Byzantine practice. He lasted eight years before being seized by a mob and brought to Heraclius' ship.

summaries_by_episode.10: A 55-year-old non-commissioned officer placed on the throne by a mutinous army, Brownworth describes him as having been 'somewhat kindly described as a sadistic monster.' His insecurity on the throne led to a bloody reign of paranoid executions, military disasters against Persia, and the introduction of mutilation and torture into Byzantine practice. He lasted eight years before being seized by a mob and brought to Heraclius' ship.

people.name: Sophronius
people.summary: The Patriarch of Jerusalem who attacked Heraclius' Christological compromise — that Christ had two natures but one energy — as heresy. His opposition caused support for the compromise to melt away precisely when unity was most needed, contributing to the collapse of Byzantine resistance to the Muslim invasion.

summaries_by_episode.10: The Patriarch of Jerusalem who attacked Heraclius' Christological compromise — that Christ had two natures but one energy — as heresy. His opposition caused support for the compromise to melt away precisely when unity was most needed, contributing to the collapse of Byzantine resistance to the Muslim invasion.

people.name: Khosrow II
people.summary: The 'great king' who used the killing of Emperor Maurice as a pretext to break the peace treaty and invade Byzantine territory, ultimately taking Mesopotamia, Armenia, Cappadocia, Jerusalem, and Egypt. Mentally unstable by the end — he had the body of his failed general Shaheen whipped beyond recognition — he was eventually overthrown and imprisoned in the Tower of Darkness, where his children were executed before him and he was slowly shot to death with arrows.

summaries_by_episode.10: The 'great king' who used the killing of Emperor Maurice as a pretext to break the peace treaty and invade Byzantine territory, ultimately taking Mesopotamia, Armenia, Cappadocia, Jerusalem, and Egypt. Mentally unstable by the end — he had the body of his failed general Shaheen whipped beyond recognition — he was eventually overthrown and imprisoned in the Tower of Darkness, where his children were executed before him and he was slowly shot to death with arrows.

people.name: Muhammad
people.summary: Brownworth notes that in 622 — the same year Heraclius set out on campaign — Muhammad fled from Mecca to Medina (the Hijra), and within five years had unleashed the armies of Islam. Muhammad died of a fever in 632, but within a hundred years his followers were at the gates of Constantinople and within 150 miles of Paris.

summaries_by_episode.10: Brownworth notes that in 622 — the same year Heraclius set out on campaign — Muhammad fled from Mecca to Medina (the Hijra), and within five years had unleashed the armies of Islam. Muhammad died of a fever in 632, but within a hundred years his followers were at the gates of Constantinople and within 150 miles of Paris.

people.name: Heraclius
