people.summary: Son of the African governor who overthrew Phocas, Heraclius came to the throne at 36 with thick curly blonde hair and a military bearing, though he had never commanded a field army. He spent 12 years reorganizing the military and finances before personally leading a brilliant campaign that crushed Persia, recovered the True Cross, and saved the empire — only to watch his life's work undone by the sudden rise of Islam. Brownworth calls him arguably the first Greek emperor, having replaced Latin with Greek as the empire's official language.

summaries_by_episode.9: Mentioned only in the closing teaser as the subject of the next episode — described as a man many thought would be the last emperor, who fought for every inch of his empire but finally ended the Persian threat.
summaries_by_episode.10: Son of the African governor who overthrew Phocas, Heraclius came to the throne at 36 with thick curly blonde hair and a military bearing, though he had never commanded a field army. He spent 12 years reorganizing the military and finances before personally leading a brilliant campaign that crushed Persia, recovered the True Cross, and saved the empire — only to watch his life's work undone by the sudden rise of Islam. Brownworth calls him arguably the first Greek emperor, having replaced Latin with Greek as the empire's official language.
summaries_by_episode.11: Brownworth briefly notes that last episode covered Heraclius, whose reign saw both euphoric triumphs and devastating tragedy. His death threw the empire into confusion, and his unpopular widow Martina's attempt to seize control was rebuffed by the people.
summaries_by_episode.13: Invoked twice as a benchmark for greatness: first as the reference point for Nikephoros Phokas being the greatest general since Heraclius, and second at the close when Brownworth says Basil II had expanded the empire more than any emperor since Heraclius. The fall of Antioch during Heraclius' reign 332 years before is also cited.

people.summary_synthesized: Son of the African governor who deposed Phocas, Heraclius came to power in the early seventh century and spent over a decade reorganizing the empire's military and finances before personally leading a campaign that crushed Persia, recovered the True Cross, and secured what many considered an extraordinary salvation of Byzantine power. He replaced Latin with Greek as the empire's official language, marking a decisive shift in imperial identity, yet his later years were overshadowed by the sudden rise of Islam, which undid much of what he had achieved. His reign became a lasting benchmark for military and territorial greatness, invoked centuries later as the standard against which later emperors were measured.

people.name: Caliph Uthman
people.summary: The third Rashidun Caliph whose assassination gave the Byzantine Empire five years of breathing room from Arab pressure. His successor, however, made clear that nothing had changed and vowed to annihilate the Roman Empire.

summaries_by_episode.11: The third Rashidun Caliph whose assassination gave the Byzantine Empire five years of breathing room from Arab pressure. His successor, however, made clear that nothing had changed and vowed to annihilate the Roman Empire.

people.name: Caliph Omar
people.summary: The second Rashidun Caliph who retook Alexandria from the Byzantines and razed its walls. He is attributed with the famous (if likely apocryphal) quote about the Library of Alexandria — that books either agreeing or disagreeing with the Quran should be destroyed — and allegedly ordered the library's books burned in public furnaces, where they took six months to consume.

summaries_by_episode.11: The second Rashidun Caliph who retook Alexandria from the Byzantines and razed its walls. He is attributed with the famous (if likely apocryphal) quote about the Library of Alexandria — that books either agreeing or disagreeing with the Quran should be destroyed — and allegedly ordered the library's books burned in public furnaces, where they took six months to consume.
summaries_by_episode.12: The Saracen leader defeated and killed by Bardas during his expeditions into Asia Minor and Armenia, marking the first blow struck against Islam's advance in almost a hundred years.

people.summary_synthesized: The second Rashidun Caliph, Omar is associated with the Arab conquest of Alexandria from the Byzantines, during which he retook the city and razed its walls; he is also attributed, likely apocryphally, with ordering the burning of the Library of Alexandria's contents in public furnaces over the course of six months. A later Caliph of the same name led Saracen expeditions into Asia Minor and Armenia before being defeated and killed by the Byzantine general Bardas. That defeat marked the first significant check on Islamic military expansion in nearly a century.

people.name: Constans II
people.summary: Feeling so threatened by pressure on the southeastern border that he took the unorthodox step of moving the imperial capital to Sicily in order to better protect Italy. He was later assassinated in his bath by a slave wielding a soap dish, after which the capital was returned to the Bosphorus.

summaries_by_episode.11: Feeling so threatened by pressure on the southeastern border that he took the unorthodox step of moving the imperial capital to Sicily in order to better protect Italy. He was later assassinated in his bath by a slave wielding a soap dish, after which the capital was returned to the Bosphorus.

people.name: Martina
people.summary: Heraclius' niece whom he married in violation of several laws and commandments after Eudosia's death, a union the Patriarch Sergius overlooked for the greater good. Never popular, she bore Heraclius nine children — four died in infancy, one had a deformed neck, another was deaf and dumb — and citizens attributed these misfortunes to God's punishment for the incestuous marriage. She became one of the most hated women in Constantinople.

summaries_by_episode.10: Heraclius' niece whom he married in violation of several laws and commandments after Eudosia's death, a union the Patriarch Sergius overlooked for the greater good. Never popular, she bore Heraclius nine children — four died in infancy, one had a deformed neck, another was deaf and dumb — and citizens attributed these misfortunes to God's punishment for the incestuous marriage. She became one of the most hated women in Constantinople.
summaries_by_episode.11: The unpopular widow of Heraclius who tried to take control of the empire after his death. The people backed a rival claimant who had her tongue cut out and her nose slit before exiling her to the island of Rhodes.

people.role: Byzantine Empress, second wife of Heraclius
people.summary_synthesized: Martina was the niece of the emperor Heraclius, whom she married after the death of his first wife Eudocia, a union condemned as incestuous and only tolerated by Patriarch Sergius for reasons of political expediency. Of their nine children, several died in infancy and others were born with physical infirmities, misfortunes the public attributed to divine punishment for the marriage, making her one of the most despised figures in Constantinople. After Heraclius died she attempted to seize control of the empire, but popular opposition backed a rival claimant, and she was mutilated—her tongue cut out and her nose slit—before being exiled to Rhodes.

people.name: Justinian II
people.summary: Mentioned as a notable example of the dynastic chaos that followed Heraclius — Justinian II was the only one of the ten emperors in that turbulent period to be overthrown twice.

summaries_by_episode.11: Mentioned as a notable example of the dynastic chaos that followed Heraclius — Justinian II was the only one of the ten emperors in that turbulent period to be overthrown twice.

people.name: Al-Walid I
people.summary: Referred to in the transcript as 'Al Malik,' Brownworth notes that this Umayyad ruler borrowed Byzantine architectural style for the Dome of the Rock, placing Byzantium's cultural reach even within the Islamic world.

summaries_by_episode.17: Referred to in the transcript as 'Al Malik,' Brownworth notes that this Umayyad ruler borrowed Byzantine architectural style for the Dome of the Rock, placing Byzantium's cultural reach even within the Islamic world.

people.name: Leo III
people.summary: A Syrian shepherd who, according to legend, caught the emperor's eye by offering sheep to feed his army on campaign, and rose through the imperial ranks to be proclaimed emperor just months before the great Arab siege of Constantinople. He outsmarted the Arab besiegers at every turn and induced the Bulgarians to deliver the killing blow to the besieging forces. Having saved the empire, he then unleashed the iconoclast controversy, ordering the destruction of holy images and causing a religious firestorm that split east from west and would drain the empire for a hundred years.

summaries_by_episode.11: A Syrian shepherd who, according to legend, caught the emperor's eye by offering sheep to feed his army on campaign, and rose through the imperial ranks to be proclaimed emperor just months before the great Arab siege of Constantinople. He outsmarted the Arab besiegers at every turn and induced the Bulgarians to deliver the killing blow to the besieging forces. Having saved the empire, he then unleashed the iconoclast controversy, ordering the destruction of holy images and causing a religious firestorm that split east from west and would drain the empire for a hundred years.

people.name: Pepin the Short
people.summary: The Frankish king who was given the title of patrician by the pope and anointed king of the Franks in exchange for military intervention against the Lombards. Pepin swept into Italy, brought the Lombards to their knees, and turned a large swath of territory over to the pope — thus creating the Papal States.

summaries_by_episode.11: The Frankish king who was given the title of patrician by the pope and anointed king of the Franks in exchange for military intervention against the Lombards. Pepin swept into Italy, brought the Lombards to their knees, and turned a large swath of territory over to the pope — thus creating the Papal States.

people.name: Constantine V
people.summary: Son of Leo III and even more extreme an iconoclast than his father — the words 'saint' or 'holy' sent him into fits of rage, and he spent much of his 34-year reign attacking monasteries with maniacal dedication, sometimes setting monks' beards on fire. By the end of his life the empire had officially been iconoclast for 50 years, and he had purged the army, government, and clergy of iconodules. He is mockingly remembered by his opponents with the nickname Copronymus, meaning 'name of dung.'

summaries_by_episode.11: Son of Leo III and even more extreme an iconoclast than his father — the words 'saint' or 'holy' sent him into fits of rage, and he spent much of his 34-year reign attacking monasteries with maniacal dedication, sometimes setting monks' beards on fire. By the end of his life the empire had officially been iconoclast for 50 years, and he had purged the army, government, and clergy of iconodules. He is mockingly remembered by his opponents with the nickname Copronymus, meaning 'name of dung.'

people.name: Charlemagne
people.summary: Son of Pepin, Charlemagne took the Frankish-papal alliance to its fullest extent. He traveled to Rome to testify on behalf of Pope Leo III, and in a stunning move the pope placed the imperial crown on his head on Christmas Day 800, declaring him Holy Roman Emperor. Charlemagne also recognized a unique opportunity in Irene's situation and proposed marriage to her, which would have reunited east and west under one ruler, but the citizens of Constantinople deposed Irene before it could happen.

summaries_by_episode.11: Son of Pepin, Charlemagne took the Frankish-papal alliance to its fullest extent. He traveled to Rome to testify on behalf of Pope Leo III, and in a stunning move the pope placed the imperial crown on his head on Christmas Day 800, declaring him Holy Roman Emperor. Charlemagne also recognized a unique opportunity in Irene's situation and proposed marriage to her, which would have reunited east and west under one ruler, but the citizens of Constantinople deposed Irene before it could happen.
summaries_by_episode.12: Mentioned as the rival emperor crowned during Irene's reign, Charlemagne was given the title Emperor of the Romans as a direct insult to the Eastern Empire, creating a rival western empire and contributing to the collapse of Byzantine prestige in the West.
summaries_by_episode.17: Brownworth uses Charlemagne as a contrast to illustrate Byzantine cultural superiority: while Charlemagne struggled to learn to write his own name, Byzantine schoolchildren were studying Homer and Aeschylus. Charlemagne also modeled his buildings in Aachen on the Imperial Palace of New Rome.

people.role: King of the Franks / Holy Roman Emperor
people.summary_synthesized: King of the Franks and son of Pepin, Charlemagne was crowned Holy Roman Emperor by Pope Leo III on Christmas Day 800, a culmination of the Frankish-papal alliance that established a rival imperial authority in the West. He briefly proposed marriage to the Byzantine empress Irene, a union that would have reunited the eastern and western empires, but her deposition ended the prospect before it could be realized. Despite the rivalry his coronation created with Constantinople, he modeled his palace at Aachen on Byzantine imperial architecture, reflecting the cultural prestige the Eastern Empire continued to hold even as the two powers competed for legitimacy.

people.name: Aistulf
people.summary: The Lombard king who captured Ravenna in 751, the last imperial stronghold in Italy, forcing the pope to seek Frankish rather than Byzantine protection.

summaries_by_episode.11: The Lombard king who captured Ravenna in 751, the last imperial stronghold in Italy, forcing the pope to seek Frankish rather than Byzantine protection.

people.name: Empress Irene
people.summary: Brownworth references Irene as the subject of the previous episode, characterizing her reign as disastrous — marked by the virtual collapse of Byzantine prestige in the West, the creation of a rival empire, and the coronation of Charlemagne as Emperor of the Romans, a direct insult to the Eastern Empire.

summaries_by_episode.12: Brownworth references Irene as the subject of the previous episode, characterizing her reign as disastrous — marked by the virtual collapse of Byzantine prestige in the West, the creation of a rival empire, and the coronation of Charlemagne as Emperor of the Romans, a direct insult to the Eastern Empire.

people.name: Irene of Athens
people.summary: A stunningly beautiful orphan from Athens chosen as wife for Leo IV, Irene proved to be one of the most grasping and ambitious women ever to be made empress. A fierce iconodule, she served as regent for her son Constantine VI and ultimately had him blinded so brutally that he died, becoming the first woman to rule the Byzantine Empire not as regent or empress but as emperor in her own right. Her reign was economically and militarily disastrous, and she was eventually deposed and exiled to Lesbos, where she died less than a year later; she is nonetheless venerated as a saint by the Orthodox Church with a feast day of August 9th.

summaries_by_episode.11: A stunningly beautiful orphan from Athens chosen as wife for Leo IV, Irene proved to be one of the most grasping and ambitious women ever to be made empress. A fierce iconodule, she served as regent for her son Constantine VI and ultimately had him blinded so brutally that he died, becoming the first woman to rule the Byzantine Empire not as regent or empress but as emperor in her own right. Her reign was economically and militarily disastrous, and she was eventually deposed and exiled to Lesbos, where she died less than a year later; she is nonetheless venerated as a saint by the Orthodox Church with a feast day of August 9th.

people.name: Leo IV
people.summary: Eldest son of Constantine V who appeared to share his father's iconoclast mindset but was weakened, likely by the tuberculosis that killed him, and weakly acquiesced to his iconodule wife Irene — allowing exiled monks to return and appointing an iconodule patriarch. He died in the fifth year of his reign leaving his ten-year-old son Constantine as heir.

summaries_by_episode.11: Eldest son of Constantine V who appeared to share his father's iconoclast mindset but was weakened, likely by the tuberculosis that killed him, and weakly acquiesced to his iconodule wife Irene — allowing exiled monks to return and appointing an iconodule patriarch. He died in the fifth year of his reign leaving his ten-year-old son Constantine as heir.

people.name: Pope Leo III
people.summary: Hated by a group of Roman noblemen who ambushed him and left him beaten unconscious in the street, then accused him of simony, perjury, drunkenness, and adultery. With no qualified authority to try him short of the empress in Constantinople — who had killed her own son and was legally disbarred by Salic law — he reached an impasse resolved by Charlemagne's testimony. He then crowned Charlemagne Holy Roman Emperor at Christmas Mass 800, a breathtaking act that effectively split the Roman Empire and allowed the papacy to claim superiority over the emperor.

summaries_by_episode.11: Hated by a group of Roman noblemen who ambushed him and left him beaten unconscious in the street, then accused him of simony, perjury, drunkenness, and adultery. With no qualified authority to try him short of the empress in Constantinople — who had killed her own son and was legally disbarred by Salic law — he reached an impasse resolved by Charlemagne's testimony. He then crowned Charlemagne Holy Roman Emperor at Christmas Mass 800, a breathtaking act that effectively split the Roman Empire and allowed the papacy to claim superiority over the emperor.

people.name: Constantine VI
people.summary: Son of Leo IV and Irene, he was only ten years old when his father died, allowing Irene to seize the regency. He proved himself hopelessly incompetent as a ruler — his one military campaign ended in him fleeing in panic, and he alienated all potential allies by divorcing his wife to marry his mistress. His mother's agents undermined his military reputation further, and Irene ultimately had him ambushed, captured, and blinded so brutally that he died, in the very room in which he had been born.

summaries_by_episode.11: Son of Leo IV and Irene, he was only ten years old when his father died, allowing Irene to seize the regency. He proved himself hopelessly incompetent as a ruler — his one military campaign ended in him fleeing in panic, and he alienated all potential allies by divorcing his wife to marry his mistress. His mother's agents undermined his military reputation further, and Irene ultimately had him ambushed, captured, and blinded so brutally that he died, in the very room in which he had been born.

people.name: Pope Nicholas I
people.summary: The pope whose claim to universal spiritual authority Photius was determined to challenge. Nicholas protested when Michael III deposed the previous patriarch and installed Photius without papal consultation; Photius responded by telling him to mind his own business. Later, when Basil deposed Photius to gain papal support, the pope had already been succeeded by a pontiff furious over Bulgaria.

summaries_by_episode.12: The pope whose claim to universal spiritual authority Photius was determined to challenge. Nicholas protested when Michael III deposed the previous patriarch and installed Photius without papal consultation; Photius responded by telling him to mind his own business. Later, when Basil deposed Photius to gain papal support, the pope had already been succeeded by a pontiff furious over Bulgaria.

people.name: Photius
people.summary: Described by Brownworth as one of the most famous scholars of the Middle Ages, Photius rose rapidly through court ranks — from captain of the guard to chief imperial secretary — before Bardas elevated him to patriarch within a week of joining the priesthood. He championed Byzantine ecclesiastical independence against Pope Nicholas I, commissioned Cyril and Methodius to evangelize the Slavs, and served as the key spiritual force behind Basil's cultural renaissance, though his political machinations around the succession of Leo nearly proved his undoing.

summaries_by_episode.12: Described by Brownworth as one of the most famous scholars of the Middle Ages, Photius rose rapidly through court ranks — from captain of the guard to chief imperial secretary — before Bardas elevated him to patriarch within a week of joining the priesthood. He championed Byzantine ecclesiastical independence against Pope Nicholas I, commissioned Cyril and Methodius to evangelize the Slavs, and served as the key spiritual force behind Basil's cultural renaissance, though his political machinations around the succession of Leo nearly proved his undoing.

people.name: Basil I
people.summary: An Armenian peasant of extraordinary physical strength and ruthless ambition, Basil rose from groom to co-emperor by ingratiating himself with Michael III and then murdering his rivals Bardas and Michael. As sole emperor he proved a transformative ruler, reconquering the Dalmatian coast and Italian cities, rebuilding the navy, reforming the law code, and adorning Constantinople with monumental buildings — leaving the empire, in Brownworth's telling, the strongest power in Europe and founding a stable dynasty that would preside over Byzantium's Golden Age.

summaries_by_episode.11: Teased at the end of the episode as the subject of the next podcast episode — Brownworth describes him as having a glittering career, overseeing a renaissance in art, restoring the glory of the empire, and founding the most splendid dynasty in Byzantine history.
summaries_by_episode.12: An Armenian peasant of extraordinary physical strength and ruthless ambition, Basil rose from groom to co-emperor by ingratiating himself with Michael III and then murdering his rivals Bardas and Michael. As sole emperor he proved a transformative ruler, reconquering the Dalmatian coast and Italian cities, rebuilding the navy, reforming the law code, and adorning Constantinople with monumental buildings — leaving the empire, in Brownworth's telling, the strongest power in Europe and founding a stable dynasty that would preside over Byzantium's Golden Age.
summaries_by_episode.13: Brownworth opens by invoking Basil I as the founder of the Macedonian dynasty, noting he came to the throne by murdering his predecessor and thus knew firsthand how a weak ruler tempted powerful generals. His military ability was never matched by his immediate successors, yet the dynasty he founded ultimately reached its greatest heights with his great-great-grandson Basil II.

people.summary_synthesized: An Armenian peasant who rose through the imperial court by cultivating the favor of Emperor Michael III, Basil I seized sole power in 867 by murdering both his rival Bardas and Michael himself. As emperor he proved a transformative ruler, reconquering the Dalmatian coast and Italian cities, rebuilding the navy, reforming the law code, and adorning Constantinople with monumental buildings. He founded the Macedonian dynasty, which would preside over Byzantium's Golden Age and reach its greatest heights two generations later under his great-great-grandson Basil II.

people.name: Empress Theodora
people.summary: Theodora ended the iconoclastic controversy by restoring the veneration of icons and was sainted for her efforts. She ruled as regent for her son Michael III until he, furious at being excluded from public life and forbidden from seeing his mistress Eudosia, overthrew her and forced her into a monastery.

summaries_by_episode.12: Theodora ended the iconoclastic controversy by restoring the veneration of icons and was sainted for her efforts. She ruled as regent for her son Michael III until he, furious at being excluded from public life and forbidden from seeing his mistress Eudosia, overthrew her and forced her into a monastery.

people.role: Byzantine Empress, regent for Michael III

people.name: Methodius
people.summary: Brother of Constantine (Cyril), commissioned by Photius alongside him to evangelize the Bulgarians, Moravians, and Rus. Though unsuccessful with the Moravians, he and his brother did their work well overall, pulling Bulgaria firmly into the Byzantine Orthodox sphere.

summaries_by_episode.12: Brother of Constantine (Cyril), commissioned by Photius alongside him to evangelize the Bulgarians, Moravians, and Rus. Though unsuccessful with the Moravians, he and his brother did their work well overall, pulling Bulgaria firmly into the Byzantine Orthodox sphere.

people.name: Theodora
people.summary: Mother of Michael III who served as regent, just as Irene had done before her, and proclaimed the final restoration of icons — bringing the iconoclast controversy to its end. Her parallel to Irene is explicitly noted by Brownworth as an example of history being written by the winners.

summaries_by_episode.11: Mother of Michael III who served as regent, just as Irene had done before her, and proclaimed the final restoration of icons — bringing the iconoclast controversy to its end. Her parallel to Irene is explicitly noted by Brownworth as an example of history being written by the winners.

people.name: Constantine (Cyril)
people.summary: Commissioned by Photius along with his brother Methodius to bring Christianity to the Bulgarians, Moravians, and Rus, Constantine took the Slavic name Cyril and, recognizing that the Slavs lacked a written language, devised the Cyrillic alphabet based on Greek characters. Though the mission to the Moravians failed, his alphabetic creation became the basis of writing systems for most of Eastern Europe.

summaries_by_episode.12: Commissioned by Photius along with his brother Methodius to bring Christianity to the Bulgarians, Moravians, and Rus, Constantine took the Slavic name Cyril and, recognizing that the Slavs lacked a written language, devised the Cyrillic alphabet based on Greek characters. Though the mission to the Moravians failed, his alphabetic creation became the basis of writing systems for most of Eastern Europe.

people.role: Byzantine monk, missionary, creator of the Cyrillic alphabet

people.name: Michael III
people.summary: Brownworth pushes back against Michael's reputation as a buffoon, noting that "there was greatness in his time," but concedes he was a weak ruler who lavished attention on horses and drinking and handed government to favorites. His capriciousness and willingness to let Basil murder his uncle Bardas ultimately sealed his own fate, as Basil turned on him next.

summaries_by_episode.11: Briefly mentioned as the emperor during whose reign the iconoclast controversy finally ended in the 9th century, and as the next subject to be discussed in the podcast series. His mother Theodora served as regent and proclaimed the restoration of icons.
summaries_by_episode.12: Brownworth pushes back against Michael's reputation as a buffoon, noting that "there was greatness in his time," but concedes he was a weak ruler who lavished attention on horses and drinking and handed government to favorites. His capriciousness and willingness to let Basil murder his uncle Bardas ultimately sealed his own fate, as Basil turned on him next.

people.summary_synthesized: Byzantine emperor in the ninth century, Michael III presided over the final resolution of the iconoclast controversy, though real power during his early reign rested with his mother Theodora, who restored the veneration of icons. As an adult ruler he proved largely ineffective, neglecting governance in favor of horses and drinking while delegating authority to favorites. His willingness to allow his favorite Basil to murder his uncle Bardas left him vulnerable to the same fate, and Basil eventually turned on him as well.

people.name: Boris I of Bulgaria
people.summary: Boris I sent ambassadors to Constantinople asking for Christian teachers, an opportunity that Photius exploited by dispatching Cyril and Methodius. He later played Rome against Constantinople over Bulgaria's ecclesiastical allegiance, but Basil outmaneuvered him by referring the question to the three eastern bishoprics, who predictably assigned Bulgaria to Constantinople.

summaries_by_episode.12: Boris I sent ambassadors to Constantinople asking for Christian teachers, an opportunity that Photius exploited by dispatching Cyril and Methodius. He later played Rome against Constantinople over Bulgaria's ecclesiastical allegiance, but Basil outmaneuvered him by referring the question to the three eastern bishoprics, who predictably assigned Bulgaria to Constantinople.

people.name: Constantine
people.summary: The only son of Basil's first wife and the one person Basil ever truly loved. His unexpected early death threw Basil into a lasting depression that bordered on insanity, and Photius exploited the emperor's grief — holding a séance and even canonizing Constantine — to block the succession of the hated Leo.

summaries_by_episode.12: The only son of Basil's first wife and the one person Basil ever truly loved. His unexpected early death threw Basil into a lasting depression that bordered on insanity, and Photius exploited the emperor's grief — holding a séance and even canonizing Constantine — to block the succession of the hated Leo.

people.role: Co-emperor, eldest son of Basil I

people.name: Leo VI
people.summary: Widely rumored to be the biological son of Michael III rather than Basil I, Leo was despised by Basil, who imprisoned and threatened to blind him after he refused to give up his mistress Zoe. Photius feared Leo's succession because it would cost him power. Brownworth strongly implies Leo was complicit in Basil's mysterious hunting death, since the rescue party was led by the father of Leo's own mistress.

Photius feared Leo's succession because it would cost him power. Brownworth strongly implies Leo was complicit in Basil's mysterious hunting death, since the rescue party was led by the father of Leo's own mistress.

people.role: Byzantine Emperor, son of Basil I

people.name: Nikephoros II Phokas
people.summary: Called by Brownworth the most superb fighter of his day and perhaps the greatest general since Belisarius, Nikephoros reconquered Crete after 136 years of Muslim rule, captured over 55 walled cities in a single season, and sacked Aleppo — earning the nickname "White Death of the Saracens." He later reconquered Tarsus, Cyprus, Aleppo, and most spectacularly Antioch, yet squandered his popularity through ruinous taxation, a disastrous western foreign policy, and his wife Theophano's conspiracy, which ended in his brutal murder in his bedroom.

summaries_by_episode.13: Called by Brownworth the most superb fighter of his day and perhaps the greatest general since Belisarius, Nikephoros reconquered Crete after 136 years of Muslim rule, captured over 55 walled cities in a single season, and sacked Aleppo — earning the nickname "White Death of the Saracens." He later reconquered Tarsus, Cyprus, Aleppo, and most spectacularly Antioch, yet squandered his popularity through ruinous taxation, a disastrous western foreign policy, and his wife Theophano's conspiracy, which ended in his brutal murder in his bedroom.

people.name: Otto I
people.summary: Mentioned briefly as the Western Emperor whose ambassadors Nikephoros Phokas thoughtlessly insulted, contributing to the domestic disaster that made his reign's brilliant military victories seem hollow.

summaries_by_episode.13: Mentioned briefly as the Western Emperor whose ambassadors Nikephoros Phokas thoughtlessly insulted, contributing to the domestic disaster that made his reign's brilliant military victories seem hollow.

people.name: Saif ad-Dawla
people.summary: The Arab ruler who had raided Byzantine territory every year for twenty years before Nikephoros Phokas launched a devastating counterattack. Brownworth recounts that Saif ad-Dawla escaped the sack of Aleppo only by scattering gold coins behind him as he fled, and was so badly shaken that he never troubled the empire again.

summaries_by_episode.13: The Arab ruler who had raided Byzantine territory every year for twenty years before Nikephoros Phokas launched a devastating counterattack. Brownworth recounts that Saif ad-Dawla escaped the sack of Aleppo only by scattering gold coins behind him as he fled, and was so badly shaken that he never troubled the empire again.

people.role: Arab ruler of Aleppo and Damascus, Hamdanid emir

people.name: Bardas Skleros
people.summary: Described as the best and brightest of Tzimiskes' generals, fiercely loyal to John but expecting to be made emperor after his death. When the popular uprising failed to materialize, he had his troops proclaim him emperor, captured the southern fleet, conquered all of Asia Minor, and besieged Constantinople from Nicaea — only to be defeated when Bardas Phokas was released from exile to fight him in a single combat duel. Exiled to Baghdad, he returned to rebel again with Caliph funding, was arrested and imprisoned by Phokas, and finally came to terms with Basil after Phokas' death — nearly blind and too old to continue.

summaries_by_episode.13: Described as the best and brightest of Tzimiskes' generals, fiercely loyal to John but expecting to be made emperor after his death. When the popular uprising failed to materialize, he had his troops proclaim him emperor, captured the southern fleet, conquered all of Asia Minor, and besieged Constantinople from Nicaea — only to be defeated when Bardas Phokas was released from exile to fight him in a single combat duel. Exiled to Baghdad, he returned to rebel again with Caliph funding, was arrested and imprisoned by Phokas, and finally came to terms with Basil after Phokas' death — nearly blind and too old to continue.

people.name: Liutprand of Cremona
people.summary: Cited by Brownworth as an example of Western contempt for Byzantium: Bishop Liutprand wrote that Byzantium was populated by 'idle liars of neither gender,' representing the kind of dismissive Western rhetoric that undermined Byzantine reputation.

summaries_by_episode.17: Cited by Brownworth as an example of Western contempt for Byzantium: Bishop Liutprand wrote that Byzantium was populated by 'idle liars of neither gender,' representing the kind of dismissive Western rhetoric that undermined Byzantine reputation.

people.name: John I Tzimiskes
people.summary: Described as one of the finest generals Byzantium ever produced, Tzimiskes led troops under Romanus II and then murdered his uncle Nikephoros Phokas with co-conspirators to seize the throne. Despite the brutality of his accession, his reign was enormously successful — repelling a Russian invasion, expelling the Russian overlord from Bulgaria, pacifying Armenia, sweeping into Mesopotamia unopposed, and returning to the east to conquer Palestine, Syria, and Lebanon. He died of poison or disease at age 51 during his eastern campaigns.

summaries_by_episode.13: Described as one of the finest generals Byzantium ever produced, Tzimiskes led troops under Romanus II and then murdered his uncle Nikephoros Phokas with co-conspirators to seize the throne. Despite the brutality of his accession, his reign was enormously successful — repelling a Russian invasion, expelling the Russian overlord from Bulgaria, pacifying Armenia, sweeping into Mesopotamia unopposed, and returning to the east to conquer Palestine, Syria, and Lebanon. He died of poison or disease at age 51 during his eastern campaigns.

people.name: Romanus II
people.summary: Basil I's great-grandson and father of Basil II, described as tall, athletic, and immensely popular, yet determined to spend his time enjoying himself rather than governing. He died after only four years, leaving behind his wife Theophano and three children — Basil, Constantine, and Anna — but his legacy was shaped more by his generals than himself.

summaries_by_episode.13: Basil I's great-grandson and father of Basil II, described as tall, athletic, and immensely popular, yet determined to spend his time enjoying himself rather than governing. He died after only four years, leaving behind his wife Theophano and three children — Basil, Constantine, and Anna — but his legacy was shaped more by his generals than himself.

people.role: Byzantine Emperor, father of Basil II

people.name: Bardas Phokas
people.summary: A giant of a man who had rebelled against John Tzimiskes and been exiled, Bardas Phokas was released from exile by the chamberlain Basil to fight Bardas Skleros — a calculated gamble that worked. He proved a superior duellist, felling Skleros in single combat, ending the civil war. Later combining forces with Skleros against Basil II, he advanced within the city walls at the Golden Horn before collapsing and dying of a stroke from his horse mid-charge during the final battle, with both armies watching — an event Brownworth frames as clear divine judgment.

summaries_by_episode.13: A giant of a man who had rebelled against John Tzimiskes and been exiled, Bardas Phokas was released from exile by the chamberlain Basil to fight Bardas Skleros — a calculated gamble that worked. He proved a superior duellist, felling Skleros in single combat, ending the civil war. Later combining forces with Skleros against Basil II, he advanced within the city walls at the Golden Horn before collapsing and dying of a stroke from his horse mid-charge during the final battle, with both armies watching — an event Brownworth frames as clear divine judgment.

people.name: Theophano
people.summary: Described by Brownworth as stunningly beautiful, completely amoral, and desperately ambitious — an innkeeper's daughter who convinced Romanus II to marry her against convention. She was immediately suspected of poisoning Romanus, then married the 51-year-old general Nikephoros Phokas, before growing bored and taking his nephew John Tzimiskes as a lover; she conspired with Tzimiskes and the chamberlain Basil to murder Nikephoros, but badly miscalculated, as John promptly sent her into exile.

summaries_by_episode.13: Described by Brownworth as stunningly beautiful, completely amoral, and desperately ambitious — an innkeeper's daughter who convinced Romanus II to marry her against convention. She was immediately suspected of poisoning Romanus, then married the 51-year-old general Nikephoros Phokas, before growing bored and taking his nephew John Tzimiskes as a lover; she conspired with Tzimiskes and the chamberlain Basil to murder Nikephoros, but badly miscalculated, as John promptly sent her into exile.

people.role: Byzantine Empress, wife of Romanus II and Nikephoros II Phokas

people.name: Basil II
people.summary: The central figure of the episode, presented by Brownworth as an emperor unlike any before or after him — possessed of boundless energy, relentless as a flow of lava, yet slow and irresistible. Spending his first 27 years as a puppet under a series of regents and usurpers, he emerged to crush two powerful rebel generals, negotiate the Varangian alliance, conquer Bulgaria in a thirty-year war, and expand the empire more than any ruler since Heraclius. He died a bachelor on December 15, 1025, having sacrificed all personal happiness to the single-minded service of the empire.

summaries_by_episode.12: Mentioned at the episode's close as Basil I's great-great-grandson who, a century later, would bring the empire to its height of prestige and glory — the subject of the next episode.
summaries_by_episode.13: The central figure of the episode, presented by Brownworth as an emperor unlike any before or after him — possessed of boundless energy, relentless as a flow of lava, yet slow and irresistible. Spending his first 27 years as a puppet under a series of regents and usurpers, he emerged to crush two powerful rebel generals, negotiate the Varangian alliance, conquer Bulgaria in a thirty-year war, and expand the empire more than any ruler since Heraclius. He died a bachelor on December 15, 1025, having sacrificed all personal happiness to the single-minded service of the empire.
summaries_by_episode.14: Described by Brownworth as the last great ruler of the Macedonian dynasty, Basil II left the empire as the largest and strongest state in Europe, with borders stretching from Italy to Armenia and from Syria to Ukraine, and an army considered invincible. His death, however, began a precipitous decline rather than an age of continued splendor, making him a soldier-emperor whose legacy was squandered by his successors.
summaries_by_episode.15: Referenced as a benchmark of Byzantine greatness; by Manuel's death the empire's prestige had been restored to a level not seen since Basil II's death.

people.summary_synthesized: Byzantine emperor from 976 to 1025 and the last great ruler of the Macedonian dynasty, Basil II spent his first decades as a puppet under regents and usurpers before emerging to crush rival generals, forge the Varangian alliance, and wage a thirty-year war of conquest against Bulgaria. At his death on December 15, 1025, the empire stretched from Italy to Armenia and from Syria to Ukraine, with an army considered invincible and a level of prestige unmatched since Heraclius. He died a bachelor, having sacrificed all personal life to the state, and the empire he left — the largest and strongest in Europe — rapidly declined under successors unable to sustain what he had built.

people.name: Samuel
people.summary: Portrayed by Brownworth as a wily and resourceful foe who outmaneuvered Basil II at first — invading Thrace, selling its population into slavery, and ambushing the Byzantine army at Trajan's Gate. He pursued a guerrilla strategy of hiding in the mountains while raiding as far south as Corinth, conquering Albania and Bosnia, but could not withstand Basil's methodical thirty-year campaign. After his entire army was blinded at the Battle of Kleidion, Samuel collapsed in shock when the mutilated prisoners were led into his presence, and died two days later a broken man.

summaries_by_episode.13: Portrayed by Brownworth as a wily and resourceful foe who outmaneuvered Basil II at first — invading Thrace, selling its population into slavery, and ambushing the Byzantine army at Trajan's Gate. He pursued a guerrilla strategy of hiding in the mountains while raiding as far south as Corinth, conquering Albania and Bosnia, but could not withstand Basil's methodical thirty-year campaign. After his entire army was blinded at the Battle of Kleidion, Samuel collapsed in shock when the mutilated prisoners were led into his presence, and died two days later a broken man.

people.name: Vladimir
people.summary: The Prince of Kiev who struck a deal with Basil II — providing 6,000 Varangian soldiers in exchange for the hand of Basil's sister Anna and his own conversion to Orthodoxy. Brownworth notes that Vladimir already had four wives and 800 concubines and was notorious for philandering, making Anna's reluctance understandable, but the Varangians arrived and proved decisive in crushing Bardas Phokas' rebellion.

summaries_by_episode.13: The Prince of Kiev who struck a deal with Basil II — providing 6,000 Varangian soldiers in exchange for the hand of Basil's sister Anna and his own conversion to Orthodoxy. Brownworth notes that Vladimir already had four wives and 800 concubines and was notorious for philandering, making Anna's reluctance understandable, but the Varangians arrived and proved decisive in crushing Bardas Phokas' rebellion.

people.name: Constantine VIII
people.summary: Mentioned by Brownworth as one of Romanus II and Theophano's sons, younger brother of Basil II, who was allowed to remain in the palace after John Tzimiskes' coup despite the uncertainty of his fate. Brownworth also notes at the end that Basil left no children, only his "worthless brother," whose successors squandered the inheritance and engineered disaster.

summaries_by_episode.13: Mentioned by Brownworth as one of Romanus II and Theophano's sons, younger brother of Basil II, who was allowed to remain in the palace after John Tzimiskes' coup despite the uncertainty of his fate. Brownworth also notes at the end that Basil left no children, only his "worthless brother," whose successors squandered the inheritance and engineered disaster.

people.role: Byzantine Emperor, brother of Basil II

people.name: Anna
people.summary: Daughter of Romanus II and Theophano, mentioned as the Byzantine bride offered by Basil II to Vladimir of Kiev in exchange for 6,000 Varangian soldiers and Vladimir's conversion to Orthodoxy. Brownworth notes she may be forgiven for being unwilling, as her future husband already had four wives and 800 concubines and was notorious for philandering, but Basil was insistent and she dutifully went.

summaries_by_episode.13: Daughter of Romanus II and Theophano, mentioned as the Byzantine bride offered by Basil II to Vladimir of Kiev in exchange for 6,000 Varangian soldiers and Vladimir's conversion to Orthodoxy. Brownworth notes she may be forgiven for being unwilling, as her future husband already had four wives and 800 concubines and was notorious for philandering, but Basil was insistent and she dutifully went.

people.role: Byzantine princess, sister of Basil II

people.name: Zoe
people.summary: Basil II's niece, sent as a royal bride to the court of Otto III in what would have been a momentous diplomatic union. She arrived only to find Otto III dead and the court in mourning, foiling the planned marriage. Brownworth speculates that had the marriage taken place, the throne of Byzantium might have fallen to her on Basil's death and east and west would have been reunited.

She arrived only to find Otto III dead and the court in mourning, foiling the planned marriage. Brownworth speculates that had the marriage taken place, the throne of Byzantium might have fallen to her on Basil's death and east and west would have been reunited.

people.role: Byzantine princess, niece of Basil II

people.name: Otto III
people.summary: Described as born of a Greek mother and dreaming of reuniting the two halves of the Roman Empire, Otto III sent senior officials to Basil II requesting a royal bride. Basil sent his niece Zoe, but when she arrived at the imperial court Otto had just died and the entire court was in mourning — a missed opportunity Brownworth calls a near-miss that could have changed subsequent history entirely.

Basil sent his niece Zoe, but when she arrived at the imperial court Otto had just died and the entire court was in mourning — a missed opportunity Brownworth calls a near-miss that could have changed subsequent history entirely.

people.name: Saint Olaf
people.summary: Mentioned only as the brother of Harald Hardrada, the most famous member of the Varangian Guard.

summaries_by_episode.13: Mentioned only as the brother of Harald Hardrada, the most famous member of the Varangian Guard.

people.name: Nikephoros III Botaneiates
people.summary: The 78-year-old general who deposed Michael VII and seized the throne, but rapidly sank into debauchery and senility, unable to deal with the problems he had inherited. He inflamed Robert Giscard by forcing Maria's daughter-in-law into a convent, and spent his reign uselessly sending Alexios to stamp out revolts while the empire decayed further. When Alexios marched on Constantinople, he abdicated and retired to a monastery.

When Alexios marched on Constantinople, he abdicated and retired to a monastery.

people.name: Harald Hardrada
people.summary: Described by Brownworth as undoubtedly the most famous member of the Varangian Guard, last of the Vikings and brother of Saint Olaf. After getting rich in Byzantine service he returned home, became King of Norway, and invaded England, dying at the hands of Harald the last Saxon king at the Battle of Stamford Bridge in 1066.

summaries_by_episode.13: Described by Brownworth as undoubtedly the most famous member of the Varangian Guard, last of the Vikings and brother of Saint Olaf. After getting rich in Byzantine service he returned home, became King of Norway, and invaded England, dying at the hands of Harald the last Saxon king at the Battle of Stamford Bridge in 1066.

people.role: Viking warrior, later King of Norway

people.name: Robert Guiscard
people.summary: Described as a brilliant Norman adventurer who arrived in Italy in 1047 with only 35 men, captured a castle in Apulia, defeated a papal army, and over the next twenty years turned southern Italy into his personal kingdom. His nickname 'the Fox' reflected his cunning; he took Bari in 1071 and most of Sicily by 1072, then set his sights on the Byzantine throne after Nikephoros forced his daughter-in-law into a convent. He invaded, won at Dyrrachium, but was lured home by Alexios-backed revolts; he returned, sacked Rome, but died of plague in Greece in 1085.

His nickname 'the Fox' reflected his cunning; he took Bari in 1071 and most of Sicily by 1072, then set his sights on the Byzantine throne after Nikephoros forced his daughter-in-law into a convent. He invaded, won at Dyrrachium, but was lured home by Alexios-backed revolts; he returned, sacked Rome, but died of plague in Greece in 1085.

people.role: Norman adventurer and Duke of Apulia

people.name: Harold
people.summary: Referenced by Brownworth as "the last Saxon king" at whose hands Harald Hardrada died at the Battle of Stamford Bridge in 1066, illustrating how far the fame and footprint of the Varangian Guard reached into European history.

summaries_by_episode.13: Referenced by Brownworth as "the last Saxon king" at whose hands Harald Hardrada died at the Battle of Stamford Bridge in 1066, illustrating how far the fame and footprint of the Varangian Guard reached into European history.

people.name: William the Conqueror
people.summary: Referenced by Brownworth as the ambitious ruler who exploited the Normans who had stayed in northern France — the illegitimate son of a duke who invaded England, killed the rightful king, and claimed the throne in 1066. He is the counterpart to Robert Giscard, representing the Norman success story of the 11th century.

summaries_by_episode.14: Referenced by Brownworth as the ambitious ruler who exploited the Normans who had stayed in northern France — the illegitimate son of a duke who invaded England, killed the rightful king, and claimed the throne in 1066. He is the counterpart to Robert Giscard, representing the Norman success story of the 11th century.

people.role: Duke of Normandy, King of England

people.name: Romanos IV Diogenes
people.summary: The emperor who led the decayed Byzantine army against the Seljuk Turks at Manzikert in 1071. Brownworth describes him as betrayed both by his commanders and by the unreliable nature of mercenaries, resulting in his army's annihilation and his own capture — a disaster that dispelled the myth of the invincible Byzantine army.

summaries_by_episode.14: The emperor who led the decayed Byzantine army against the Seljuk Turks at Manzikert in 1071. Brownworth describes him as betrayed both by his commanders and by the unreliable nature of mercenaries, resulting in his army's annihilation and his own capture — a disaster that dispelled the myth of the invincible Byzantine army.

people.name: Urban II
people.summary: Elevated to the papal throne in 1088, Urban II is described as a man of considerable diplomatic flexibility who lifted the excommunication on the Byzantine emperor as his first act. When Alexios wrote asking for mercenaries against the Turks, Urban seized the opportunity — delivering at the Council of Clermont what Brownworth calls 'undoubtedly the single most effective speech ever heard in Europe,' calling for a crusade to Jerusalem rather than the few hundred mercenaries Alexios had requested.

summaries_by_episode.14: Elevated to the papal throne in 1088, Urban II is described as a man of considerable diplomatic flexibility who lifted the excommunication on the Byzantine emperor as his first act. When Alexios wrote asking for mercenaries against the Turks, Urban seized the opportunity — delivering at the Council of Clermont what Brownworth calls 'undoubtedly the single most effective speech ever heard in Europe,' calling for a crusade to Jerusalem rather than the few hundred mercenaries Alexios had requested.

people.name: Alexios Komnenos
people.summary: The episode's central figure, Alexios came to the throne at 24 amid an empire shattered by military defeat, fiscal ruin, and territorial collapse. Brownworth frames him as a supremely gifted statesman and diplomat who, lacking a competent army, stabilized the empire through intrigue, bribery, and playing enemies against each other — defeating the Normans twice, the Pechenegs, Cumans, and managing the First Crusade with masterly duplicity. His forty years on the throne saved the empire, though his system of personal diplomacy was too complex to survive him.

summaries_by_episode.13: Mentioned only in the closing teaser as the subject of the next episode — the emperor who had to pick up all the pieces after the decline following Basil II, appealed to the West for help, and got instead the Crusades.

Brownworth frames him as a supremely gifted statesman and diplomat who, lacking a competent army, stabilized the empire through intrigue, bribery, and playing enemies against each other — defeating the Normans twice, the Pechenegs, Cumans, and managing the First Crusade with masterly duplicity. His forty years on the throne saved the empire, though his system of personal diplomacy was too complex to survive him.
summaries_by_episode.17: Brownworth notes that Alexios I wondered with good reason whether he had more to fear from the Turks or from the Crusaders, illustrating the dangerous ambivalence at the heart of Byzantine-Western relations during the First Crusade.

people.role: Byzantine Emperor (teased as next episode subject)
people.summary_synthesized: Alexios I Komnenos became Byzantine emperor at age 24, inheriting an empire shattered by military defeat, fiscal ruin, and severe territorial loss. Lacking a reliable army, he stabilized the realm through diplomacy, bribery, and calculated manipulation of enemies — defeating the Normans twice and neutralizing the Pechenegs and Cumans — while navigating the dangerous arrival of the First Crusade, which answered his appeal to the West for military aid in far greater and more unpredictable force than he had sought. His forty years on the throne preserved the empire, though the system of personal statecraft he employed was too intricate to outlast him.

people.name: Henry IV
people.summary: Referenced as the Holy Roman Emperor who was already wary of Robert Giscard's growing power in southern Italy. Alexios exploited this by pointing out the threat of Robert's overseas expansion; Robert subsequently drove the German Emperor from Italy and carried out a brutal three-day sack of Rome in the course of returning to deal with the Alexios-backed Norman revolt.

summaries_by_episode.14: Referenced as the Holy Roman Emperor who was already wary of Robert Giscard's growing power in southern Italy. Alexios exploited this by pointing out the threat of Robert's overseas expansion; Robert subsequently drove the German Emperor from Italy and carried out a brutal three-day sack of Rome in the course of returning to deal with the Alexios-backed Norman revolt.

people.name: Michael VII Doukas
people.summary: Described as the incompetent emperor who, in the face of the Turkish advance in 1078, officially recognized the Turks' hold on Asia Minor in exchange for help against a renegade mercenary. He also sent Alexios away to the east, and was subsequently deposed in a civil war, abdicating in favor of Nikephoros Botaneiates.

summaries_by_episode.14: Described as the incompetent emperor who, in the face of the Turkish advance in 1078, officially recognized the Turks' hold on Asia Minor in exchange for help against a renegade mercenary. He also sent Alexios away to the east, and was subsequently deposed in a civil war, abdicating in favor of Nikephoros Botaneiates.

people.name: Peter the Hermit
people.summary: The fanatical peasant leader who led 40,000 French and German peasants into Byzantine territory as the first crusading army. Brownworth describes him as unable to control his followers — his mob burned Belgrade, tried to loot a neighboring city, had 10,000 men killed by Byzantine cavalry, then looted the suburbs of Constantinople. Ferried across the Bosphorus, Peter ignored Alexios' advice not to engage the Turks, got his rabble wiped out, and slunk back to Constantinople in disgrace.

Ferried across the Bosphorus, Peter ignored Alexios' advice not to engage the Turks, got his rabble wiped out, and slunk back to Constantinople in disgrace.

people.name: Maria of Alania
people.summary: The beautiful 29-year-old wife of deposed Michael VII whom the nearly 80-year-old Nikephoros Botaneiates married — a move Brownworth describes as bad enough given that her former husband was still alive. She later charmed Alexios and adopted him into the royal family, providing him the semblance of legitimacy he needed to march on Constantinople.

summaries_by_episode.14: The beautiful 29-year-old wife of deposed Michael VII whom the nearly 80-year-old Nikephoros Botaneiates married — a move Brownworth describes as bad enough given that her former husband was still alive. She later charmed Alexios and adopted him into the royal family, providing him the semblance of legitimacy he needed to march on Constantinople.

people.name: Bohemond of Taranto
people.summary: Robert Giscard's son whom Robert left in charge of the Balkans campaign after being recalled to Italy. Bohemond was outmatched by Alexios at every turn — harassed, bribed, and finally driven to a humiliating surrender. He later led one of the First Crusade armies, took Antioch, and became its lord — a bitter blow to Alexios who had lost the city. Captured by the Turks, convinced Alexios was to blame, he invaded a second time only to be defeated by Alexios' diplomacy without a battle, and died shortly after in obscurity.

people.role: Norman prince, leader of the First Crusade, Prince of Antioch

people.name: Alexius I Komnenos
people.summary: After the shattering defeat at Manzikert, Alexius rebuilt Byzantine power while being pressured from both East and West. He granted Venice expansive trading rights in exchange for use of her navy to fend off a Norman invasion, and managed the armies of the First Crusade passing through his dominions. Andronicus I was his grandson.

summaries_by_episode.15: After the shattering defeat at Manzikert, Alexius rebuilt Byzantine power while being pressured from both East and West. He granted Venice expansive trading rights in exchange for use of her navy to fend off a Norman invasion, and managed the armies of the First Crusade passing through his dominions. Andronicus I was his grandson.

people.name: Hugh of Vermandois
alt_names: son of the King of France
people.summary: Mentioned by Brownworth only as 'the son of the King of France' among the impressive list of crusader leaders who passed through Constantinople — each desperate to be appointed head of the crusade, all played off against each other by Alexios.

summaries_by_episode.14: Mentioned by Brownworth only as 'the son of the King of France' among the impressive list of crusader leaders who passed through Constantinople — each desperate to be appointed head of the crusade, all played off against each other by Alexios.

people.name: Baldwin I
people.summary: Mentioned as a crusader leader who, when summoned to Alexios' throne room, brought his knights along, and one of them — in flagrant violation of etiquette — had the audacity to sit in the emperor's throne and boast he had never met his match in battle. Brownworth notes that Baldwin would later become King of Jerusalem.

Brownworth notes that Baldwin would later become King of Jerusalem.

people.role: Crusader leader, later King of Jerusalem

people.name: William IX of Aquitaine
people.summary: Mentioned by Brownworth as 'the son of William the Conqueror' among the crusader leaders who arrived at Constantinople, each wanting to be named head of the crusade. Alexios skillfully played them off against each other while dangling the possibility that he himself might join the crusade.

summaries_by_episode.14: Mentioned by Brownworth as 'the son of William the Conqueror' among the crusader leaders who arrived at Constantinople, each wanting to be named head of the crusade. Alexios skillfully played them off against each other while dangling the possibility that he himself might join the crusade.

people.name: Suleiman ibn Qutalmish
people.summary: Referenced as the Sultan of Rome (Rum) who assumed the first crusading army could be dealt with as easily as Peter the Hermit's rabble, confidently attacking as they besieged his capital of Nicaea. To his horror he discovered these soldiers were nothing like Peter's mob, his army broke and fled before the heavily armed crusader knights, and he offered no further resistance.

To his horror he discovered these soldiers were nothing like Peter's mob, his army broke and fled before the heavily armed crusader knights, and he offered no further resistance.

people.role: Sultan of the Seljuk Sultanate of Rum

people.name: John II Komnenos
people.summary: Son of Alexius I, John the Beautiful built on his father's accomplishments in restoring imperial prestige. He attempted to break Venetian trading dominance by refusing to renew his father's concessions, but without an imperial navy and facing spiraling costs, the resulting conflict proved not worth fighting.

summaries_by_episode.15: Son of Alexius I, John the Beautiful built on his father's accomplishments in restoring imperial prestige. He attempted to break Venetian trading dominance by refusing to renew his father's concessions, but without an imperial navy and facing spiraling costs, the resulting conflict proved not worth fighting.

people.name: Pope Gregory VIII
people.summary: The Pope who allegedly died of shock when he heard of the fall of Jerusalem, and whose successor immediately preached a new crusade. Brownworth references him as a measure of how devastating the news of Jerusalem's fall was in Europe.

summaries_by_episode.15: The Pope who allegedly died of shock when he heard of the fall of Jerusalem, and whose successor immediately preached a new crusade. Brownworth references him as a measure of how devastating the news of Jerusalem's fall was in Europe.

people.name: Enrico Dandolo
people.summary: The remarkable Doge of Venice, in his middle 80s and completely blind but possessing boundless energy and immense shrewdness. He masterminded the diversion of the Fourth Crusade, first to Zara and then to Constantinople, cleverly exploiting the crusaders' debt to Venice and welcoming the fugitive Alexius Angelus as a tool to capture the great city. He personally turned the tide at the sea walls of Constantinople by leaping from his ship and planting the banner of St. Mark. He was buried in the Hagia Sophia, and in 1453 the Turks mistook his tomb for a saint's and threw his bones to the dogs.

He masterminded the diversion of the Fourth Crusade, first to Zara and then to Constantinople, cleverly exploiting the crusaders' debt to Venice and welcoming the fugitive Alexius Angelus as a tool to capture the great city. He personally turned the tide at the sea walls of Constantinople by leaping from his ship and planting the banner of St. Mark. He was buried in the Hagia Sophia, and in 1453 the Turks mistook his tomb for a saint's and threw his bones to the dogs.

people.name: Andronicus I Komnenos
people.summary: A cousin of Manuel I and grandson of Alexius I, Andronicus was dashing, eloquent, and physically imposing at 6'4". He exploited popular resentment of western influence to seize the throne, then massacred Italians in Constantinople, eliminated rival claimants, strangled the young Alexius II, and married his 13-year-old widow. Though his anti-corruption campaign brought some relief, his reign of terror and paranoid violence brought the empire to crisis, and he was overthrown, tortured, and killed by the mob in 1185.

summaries_by_episode.15: A cousin of Manuel I and grandson of Alexius I, Andronicus was dashing, eloquent, and physically imposing at 6'4". He exploited popular resentment of western influence to seize the throne, then massacred Italians in Constantinople, eliminated rival claimants, strangled the young Alexius II, and married his 13-year-old widow. Though his anti-corruption campaign brought some relief, his reign of terror and paranoid violence brought the empire to crisis, and he was overthrown, tortured, and killed by the mob in 1185.

people.name: Manuel I Komnenos
people.summary: Grandson of Alexius I, Manuel restored imperial prestige to a level not seen since Basil II, making Byzantium the unquestioned secular leader of the Christian world at his death. He approached Genoa and Pisa with trading offers, then arrested every Venetian in the empire and confiscated their property, earning dangerous Venetian animosity without building the imperial navy to compensate. His marriage to Maria of Antioch and interest in western customs proved deeply unpopular.

summaries_by_episode.15: Grandson of Alexius I, Manuel restored imperial prestige to a level not seen since Basil II, making Byzantium the unquestioned secular leader of the Christian world at his death. He approached Genoa and Pisa with trading offers, then arrested every Venetian in the empire and confiscated their property, earning dangerous Venetian animosity without building the imperial navy to compensate. His marriage to Maria of Antioch and interest in western customs proved deeply unpopular.

people.name: Frederick Barbarossa
people.summary: The Holy Roman Emperor who led the Third Crusade by land through Byzantine territory with an immense army of 150,000. He had himself crowned King of the Romans and was no friend to Byzantium, having been opposed by Byzantine-backed Italian rebels. Isaac II mishandled his passage disastrously—cutting off food, attacking his forces, and imprisoning his ambassadors—until Frederick threatened to crusade against Byzantium itself. He drowned crossing the Chalycadnus River in Seleucia before reaching the Holy Land, and his army immediately disintegrated.

summaries_by_episode.15: The Holy Roman Emperor who led the Third Crusade by land through Byzantine territory with an immense army of 150,000. He had himself crowned King of the Romans and was no friend to Byzantium, having been opposed by Byzantine-backed Italian rebels. Isaac II mishandled his passage disastrously—cutting off food, attacking his forces, and imprisoning his ambassadors—until Frederick threatened to crusade against Byzantium itself. He drowned crossing the Chalycadnus River in Seleucia before reaching the Holy Land, and his army immediately disintegrated.

people.name: Alexius Murtzuphlus
people.summary: Son-in-law of Alexius III and described by Brownworth as perhaps the only effective player on the Byzantine stage during the Fourth Crusade crisis. He was sent by Alexius IV to beg the crusaders for help, but instead seized the moment—bursting into the emperor's bedroom, feigning urgency, and leading him to conspirators who imprisoned him alongside his father. Murtzuphlus then had himself crowned Alexius V, repaired the walls, and stiffened resistance to the crusaders, but ultimately fled when the city was breached. He was later captured and flung from the top of the Theodosian column in the Hippodrome.

He was sent by Alexius IV to beg the crusaders for help, but instead seized the moment—bursting into the emperor's bedroom, feigning urgency, and leading him to conspirators who imprisoned him alongside his father. Murtzuphlus then had himself crowned Alexius V, repaired the walls, and stiffened resistance to the crusaders, but ultimately fled when the city was breached. He was later captured and flung from the top of the Theodosian column in the Hippodrome.

people.name: Maria of Antioch
people.summary: A westerner from Antioch who married Emperor Manuel I and became the deeply unpopular regent for their 11-year-old son Alexius II after Manuel's death. Her government's restoration of Venetian trading privileges and use of Italian and Frankish merchants sparked riots and anti-western sentiment in Constantinople. Andronicus ultimately forced Alexius II to sign his own mother's death warrant.

Her government's restoration of Venetian trading privileges and use of Italian and Frankish merchants sparked riots and anti-western sentiment in Constantinople. Andronicus ultimately forced Alexius II to sign his own mother's death warrant.

people.name: Alexius III Angelos
people.summary: The older brother of Isaac II who took the opportunity of Isaac's hunting expedition to depose him, blind him, and imprison him along with his son. His reign was characterized by emptying the treasury on gardens and lavish parties while the Turks overran the eastern frontiers and Bulgaria ravaged Macedonia and Thrace. When the Fourth Crusade arrived, he was too paralyzed to act and ultimately fled Constantinople grabbing his favorite daughter and the palace treasure, leaving the city to fend for itself.

summaries_by_episode.15: The older brother of Isaac II who took the opportunity of Isaac's hunting expedition to depose him, blind him, and imprison him along with his son. His reign was characterized by emptying the treasury on gardens and lavish parties while the Turks overran the eastern frontiers and Bulgaria ravaged Macedonia and Thrace. When the Fourth Crusade arrived, he was too paralyzed to act and ultimately fled Constantinople grabbing his favorite daughter and the palace treasure, leaving the city to fend for itself.

people.name: William II of Sicily
people.summary: The King of Sicily, whose Greek court welcomed Byzantine refugees fleeing Andronicus's reign of terror. Using a young man claiming to be the murdered Alexius II as a pretext, he assembled an army 80,000 strong and invaded, sweeping across the Balkans and sacking Thessalonica in just nine days before being turned back by Isaac's revitalized forces.

summaries_by_episode.15: The King of Sicily, whose Greek court welcomed Byzantine refugees fleeing Andronicus's reign of terror. Using a young man claiming to be the murdered Alexius II as a pretext, he assembled an army 80,000 strong and invaded, sweeping across the Balkans and sacking Thessalonica in just nine days before being turned back by Isaac's revitalized forces.

people.name: Isaac II Angelos
people.summary: Mentioned only in the closing teaser as the subject of the next episode — described as a man possessing none of Alexios' skills, who became a pawn of the Venetians while the empire paid the price, and during whose reign Constantinople itself fell victim to the soldiers of the Fourth Crusade.

summaries_by_episode.14: Mentioned only in the closing teaser as the subject of the next episode — described as a man possessing none of Alexios' skills, who became a pawn of the Venetians while the empire paid the price, and during whose reign Constantinople itself fell victim to the soldiers of the Fourth Crusade.

people.name: Isaac II Angelos
people.summary: A cousin of Andronicus who seized the throne in 1185 after killing the officer sent to arrest him and rallying the people at the Hagia Sophia. His reign began promisingly with a victory over the Sicilians, but descended into reckless spending, crushing taxes, sold offices, and military failure in Bulgaria. He was deposed, blinded, and imprisoned by his own brother Alexius, and eventually died in prison after being briefly restored as a broken co-emperor alongside his son Alexius IV.

summaries_by_episode.15: A cousin of Andronicus who seized the throne in 1185 after killing the officer sent to arrest him and rallying the people at the Hagia Sophia. His reign began promisingly with a victory over the Sicilians, but descended into reckless spending, crushing taxes, sold offices, and military failure in Bulgaria. He was deposed, blinded, and imprisoned by his own brother Alexius, and eventually died in prison after being briefly restored as a broken co-emperor alongside his son Alexius IV.

people.name: Richard I of England
people.summary: King of England who participated in the Third Crusade, sailing to the Holy Land and bypassing Byzantine territory. Upon leaving the Holy Land at the crusade's end, he announced that Egypt was the weak point of the Muslim East and all future crusades should be focused there—advice that Dandolo later used to manipulate the leaders of the Fourth Crusade.

summaries_by_episode.15: King of England who participated in the Third Crusade, sailing to the Holy Land and bypassing Byzantine territory. Upon leaving the Holy Land at the crusade's end, he announced that Egypt was the weak point of the Muslim East and all future crusades should be focused there—advice that Dandolo later used to manipulate the leaders of the Fourth Crusade.

people.name: Philip Augustus
people.summary: King of France who declared himself ready to go on the Third Crusade alongside Frederick Barbarossa and Richard the Lion-Hearted. He chose to sail to the Holy Land rather than march overland through Byzantine territory, thereby bypassing imperial lands altogether.

summaries_by_episode.15: King of France who declared himself ready to go on the Third Crusade alongside Frederick Barbarossa and Richard the Lion-Hearted. He chose to sail to the Holy Land rather than march overland through Byzantine territory, thereby bypassing imperial lands altogether.

people.name: Alexius II Komnenos
people.summary: The 11-year-old son of Manuel I for whom Maria of Antioch served as regent. Andronicus forced him to sign his own mother's death warrant, then had himself crowned co-emperor alongside the boy; two months later, Andronicus had Alexius strangled and his body thrown into the Bosphorus. A pretender impersonating him was later used by William of Sicily as a pretext for invasion.

summaries_by_episode.15: The 11-year-old son of Manuel I for whom Maria of Antioch served as regent. Andronicus forced him to sign his own mother's death warrant, then had himself crowned co-emperor alongside the boy; two months later, Andronicus had Alexius strangled and his body thrown into the Bosphorus. A pretender impersonating him was later used by William of Sicily as a pretext for invasion.

people.name: Alexius IV Angelos
people.summary: Son of Isaac II who escaped from prison and headed west to find support to overthrow his uncle Alexius III. He found it through Enrico Dandolo and the Fourth Crusade, promising to end the Orthodox-Catholic schism, pay the crusader debts, and provide military support. Crowned co-emperor alongside his restored father, he could fulfill none of his extravagant promises, stripping church plate and raising crushing taxes, until Alexius Murtzuphlus seized him and strangled him with a bowstring.

summaries_by_episode.15: Son of Isaac II who escaped from prison and headed west to find support to overthrow his uncle Alexius III. He found it through Enrico Dandolo and the Fourth Crusade, promising to end the Orthodox-Catholic schism, pay the crusader debts, and provide military support. Crowned co-emperor alongside his restored father, he could fulfill none of his extravagant promises, stripping church plate and raising crushing taxes, until Alexius Murtzuphlus seized him and strangled him with a bowstring.

people.name: Baldwin II
people.summary: The last Latin emperor, described by Brownworth as pathetic — he fled the city on a Venetian ship when Michael VIII's Caesar entered the gates, an ignominious end to the bumbling Latin Empire.

summaries_by_episode.16: The last Latin emperor, described by Brownworth as pathetic — he fled the city on a Venetian ship when Michael VIII's Caesar entered the gates, an ignominious end to the bumbling Latin Empire.

people.name: Michael VIII
people.summary: Brownworth describes Michael VIII as the emperor who recaptured Constantinople from the Latin Empire in 1261, entering through an unlocked gate with a handful of men and being crowned in the Hagia Sophia. Though he vowed to restore the empire, Brownworth says it was already too late to salvage its fortunes.

summaries_by_episode.16: Brownworth describes Michael VIII as the emperor who recaptured Constantinople from the Latin Empire in 1261, entering through an unlocked gate with a handful of men and being crowned in the Hagia Sophia. Though he vowed to restore the empire, Brownworth says it was already too late to salvage its fortunes.

people.name: Othman
people.summary: Brownworth describes Othman as an extraordinary warrior who united several Turkic tribes under jihad, conquered Ephesus, and captured Bursa just across from Constantinople, making it his capital with undisguised ambitions on the city itself. He died before taking Constantinople, leaving that goal to his sons, and the Turks named themselves 'Ottoman' in his honor.

summaries_by_episode.16: Brownworth describes Othman as an extraordinary warrior who united several Turkic tribes under jihad, conquered Ephesus, and captured Bursa just across from Constantinople, making it his capital with undisguised ambitions on the city itself. He died before taking Constantinople, leaving that goal to his sons, and the Turks named themselves 'Ottoman' in his honor.

people.name: Giotto di Bondone
people.summary: Brownworth lists Giotto among diverse painters inspired by Byzantine mosaics scattered throughout the churches of the Byzantine world, illustrating how Byzantine art seeded the Western artistic tradition.

summaries_by_episode.17: Brownworth lists Giotto among diverse painters inspired by Byzantine mosaics scattered throughout the churches of the Byzantine world, illustrating how Byzantine art seeded the Western artistic tradition.

people.name: John Kantakouzenos
people.summary: Brownworth uses John Kantakouzenos as a vivid illustration of how far the Byzantine Empire had drifted from classical Roman identity by its final centuries: visiting Europe calling himself a Roman Emperor while dressed in turban-like headgear, slippers, attended by eunuchs, and performing 'effeminate Eastern ceremonies.' This image, Brownworth argues, made later observers judge the empire as something wholly un-Roman.

summaries_by_episode.1: Brownworth uses John Kantakouzenos as a vivid illustration of how far the Byzantine Empire had drifted from classical Roman identity by its final centuries: visiting Europe calling himself a Roman Emperor while dressed in turban-like headgear, slippers, attended by eunuchs, and performing 'effeminate Eastern ceremonies.' This image, Brownworth argues, made later observers judge the empire as something wholly un-Roman.

people.name: John VI
people.summary: Brownworth uses the coronation of John VI in 1347 as a snapshot of imperial decline — the crown jewels used in the ceremony were obviously made of glass since the real ones were in pawn to Venice, and the banquet was served on humble pewter and clay.
