places.summary_synthesized: Located in Asia Minor across the water from Constantinople, Nicaea was the site of the first ecumenical council of the Christian church in 325, where the homoousios formula defining Christ's nature was adopted. The city recurred as a strategic military base across centuries, serving as a staging ground for sieges of Constantinople, the capital of the Seljuk Sultanate of Rum until its quiet surrender to Byzantium during the First Crusade, and the seat of the Byzantine government-in-exile from which Michael VIII launched his successful reconquest of Constantinople in 1261. It eventually fell to the Ottomans as part of their consolidation of Asia Minor following Othman's death.

places.name: Nicomedia
places.summary: The city where the army, withdrawing from Persia, broke open Numerian's carriage and discovered his decomposed body. It was also Diocletian's preferred eastern capital and the site where he publicly accused and executed Aper.

summaries_by_episode.2: The city where the army, withdrawing from Persia, broke open Numerian's carriage and discovered his decomposed body. It was also Diocletian's preferred eastern capital and the site where he publicly accused and executed Aper.
summaries_by_episode.4: The eastern city where Arius fled after his excommunication in Alexandria and received a warm welcome from Emperor Licinius. A synod called by the local bishop Eusebius overwhelmingly endorsed Arius there.
summaries_by_episode.5: The city to which Julian was packed off after his family was massacred, to receive a Christian education under the bishop Eusebius.
summaries_by_episode.16: Mentioned by Brownworth as one of the cities that fell to the Ottomans in rapid succession after Othman's death, leaving virtually nothing of the Byzantine empire in Asia.

places.summary_synthesized: A prominent city in northwestern Asia Minor, Nicomedia served as Diocletian's preferred eastern capital and was the site where he publicly accused and executed the praetorian prefect Aper after troops discovered the decomposed body of Emperor Numerian. The city later became a refuge for the exiled theologian Arius, where a synod convened by Bishop Eusebius endorsed his teachings, and subsequently the place where the future emperor Julian was sent to receive a Christian education following the massacre of his family. It eventually fell to the Ottomans in the rapid territorial collapse that left Byzantium with virtually no remaining presence in Asia Minor.

places.name: Nineveh
places.summary: The ancient city where the decisive battle of the Byzantine-Persian war was fought in 627. Both sides chose the ground knowing it was the decisive conflict. The battle lasted eleven hours; Heraclius reportedly challenged the Persian general to single combat and killed him, and by day's end virtually no Persians were left alive.

summaries_by_episode.10: The ancient city where the decisive battle of the Byzantine-Persian war was fought in 627. Both sides chose the ground knowing it was the decisive conflict. The battle lasted eleven hours; Heraclius reportedly challenged the Persian general to single combat and killed him, and by day's end virtually no Persians were left alive.

places.name: North Africa
places.summary: Brownworth argues that the stoutness of Constantinople's walls forced the armies of Islam to take the long way through North Africa and Spain, overextending their resources and giving Europe time to resist — making Byzantine endurance the decisive factor in Western survival.

summaries_by_episode.4: Mentioned as the route Islamic armies were forced to take because the walls of Constantinople blocked their path, ultimately leading them through North Africa and Spain before being stopped at the Battle of Tours.
summaries_by_episode.8: North Africa was the territory of the Vandal Kingdom, which Justinian targeted first as a test for Belisarius before the main Italian campaign. Belisarius reconquered it in little more than a year with only 15,000 men, extinguishing the Vandal kingdom.
summaries_by_episode.17: Brownworth argues that the stoutness of Constantinople's walls forced the armies of Islam to take the long way through North Africa and Spain, overextending their resources and giving Europe time to resist — making Byzantine endurance the decisive factor in Western survival.

places.summary_synthesized: A coastal region stretching across the southern Mediterranean, North Africa served as the territory of the Vandal Kingdom until Belisarius reconquered it for the Byzantine Empire in little more than a year with roughly 15,000 men, extinguishing Vandal rule entirely. It later became the route Islamic armies were compelled to take after the walls of Constantinople blocked a direct westward advance, leading their expansion through Spain before it was halted at the Battle of Tours. This long detour overextended their resources and gave Western Europe time to mount resistance.

places.name: Northern Syria
places.summary: The region where Alexios sent a modest force to oust Bohemond from some ports after Antioch became a crusader state, and also where Bohemond had previously been captured by the Turks and thrown into a Turkish prison for three years.

summaries_by_episode.14: The region where Alexios sent a modest force to oust Bohemond from some ports after Antioch became a crusader state, and also where Bohemond had previously been captured by the Turks and thrown into a Turkish prison for three years.

places.name: Ostia
places.summary: The port city attacked by Isaac out of boredom in violation of Belisarius' strict orders. Isaac's capture there caused Belisarius to abort the amphibious attack on Rome and rush back to camp, squandering the last real chance to save the city.

summaries_by_episode.9: The port city attacked by Isaac out of boredom in violation of Belisarius' strict orders. Isaac's capture there caused Belisarius to abort the amphibious attack on Rome and rush back to camp, squandering the last real chance to save the city.

places.name: Otranto
places.summary: An Italian coastal city reconquered by Basil I, which along with Bari provided an excellent bridgehead from which Nikephoros Phokas would later conquer virtually all of southern Italy.

summaries_by_episode.12: An Italian coastal city reconquered by Basil I, which along with Bari provided an excellent bridgehead from which Nikephoros Phokas would later conquer virtually all of southern Italy.

places.name: Palermo
places.summary: Palermo was the only Gothic resistance in Sicily. Belisarius overcame it by sailing his ships up to the city walls, hoisting his men to the tops of the masts, and firing down on the defenders from above.

summaries_by_episode.8: Palermo was the only Gothic resistance in Sicily. Belisarius overcame it by sailing his ships up to the city walls, hoisting his men to the tops of the masts, and firing down on the defenders from above.

places.name: Palestine
places.summary: Conquered by John Tzimiskes in the final year of his eastern campaigns, alongside Syria and Lebanon, as well as the major coastal cities except Tripoli.

summaries_by_episode.13: Conquered by John Tzimiskes in the final year of his eastern campaigns, alongside Syria and Lebanon, as well as the major coastal cities except Tripoli.

places.name: Papal States
places.summary: The territory brought into existence when Pepin the Short turned over to the pope the lands he had conquered from the Lombards. Brownworth notes that in a vastly shrunken form these still exist today as Vatican City.

summaries_by_episode.11: The territory brought into existence when Pepin the Short turned over to the pope the lands he had conquered from the Lombards. Brownworth notes that in a vastly shrunken form these still exist today as Vatican City.

places.name: Paris
places.summary: Mentioned as a measure of the staggering reach of Islamic expansion: within 150 years of Muhammad's death, Muslim forces were within 150 miles of Paris in the west, having swept across North Africa and into Europe.

summaries_by_episode.5: Where Julian stopped to rest while reluctantly making his way east with troops for Constantius, and where the Petulantes Legion mutinied and declared him emperor.
summaries_by_episode.10: Mentioned as a measure of the staggering reach of Islamic expansion: within 150 years of Muhammad's death, Muslim forces were within 150 miles of Paris in the west, having swept across North Africa and into Europe.

places.summary_synthesized: A city in western Europe, Paris serves as a geographic marker for two pivotal moments in late antique and early medieval history. It was the site where the Petulantes Legion mutinied and declared Julian emperor while he was reluctantly leading troops eastward for Constantius. Centuries later, it stood as a measure of Islamic expansion's reach, with Muslim forces advancing to within 150 miles of the city less than 150 years after Muhammad's death.

places.name: Parthenon (Church of the Mother of God)
places.summary: Referred to by Brownworth as the "Church of the Mother of God, better known as the Parthenon," where Basil II attended a service at the end of his Bulgarian campaign — indicating the building had been converted to Christian use by this period.

summaries_by_episode.13: Referred to by Brownworth as the "Church of the Mother of God, better known as the Parthenon," where Basil II attended a service at the end of his Bulgarian campaign — indicating the building had been converted to Christian use by this period.

places.name: Peloponnese
places.summary: The last Byzantine stronghold outside the walls of the capital, given to Constantine XI as a young man to govern. He successfully restored it to full Byzantine control and even pushed into northern Greece before the Sultan sent troops to teach him a lesson, destroying the Hexamilion and barely letting him escape with his life.

summaries_by_episode.16: The last Byzantine stronghold outside the walls of the capital, given to Constantine XI as a young man to govern. He successfully restored it to full Byzantine control and even pushed into northern Greece before the Sultan sent troops to teach him a lesson, destroying the Hexamilion and barely letting him escape with his life.

places.name: Persia
places.summary: The Persians recovered from the plague in 544 and launched an invasion of Byzantine Mesopotamia, extracting 28,000 gold pieces per year to stand down. Brownworth presents the Persian front as one of several simultaneous crises draining the empire during the Italian campaign.

summaries_by_episode.2: The eastern rival against which Rome fought chronic wars. Carus led an expedition there in 283, sacking the Persian capital, before dying on the banks of the Tigris; Brownworth notes these eastern campaigns were a perpetual drain on Roman resources and attention.
summaries_by_episode.7: Rome's ancient eastern enemy, described by Brownworth as always threatening to reignite chronic and inevitable war with the Byzantine Empire. The Persian threat was one of several pressures making the empire's survival look doubtful in the generation before Justinian.
summaries_by_episode.9: The Persians recovered from the plague in 544 and launched an invasion of Byzantine Mesopotamia, extracting 28,000 gold pieces per year to stand down. Brownworth presents the Persian front as one of several simultaneous crises draining the empire during the Italian campaign.

places.summary_synthesized: Persia was Rome's principal eastern rival, a persistent adversary against which both Roman and later Byzantine emperors fought recurring, resource-draining wars stretching from at least the third century onward. The Roman emperor Carus sacked the Persian capital in 283 before dying on the Tigris, yet the conflict remained unresolved, and Persia continued to threaten the Byzantine Empire's eastern frontier into the era of Justinian. In 544, recovering from plague, the Persians invaded Byzantine Mesopotamia and extracted 28,000 gold pieces annually to withdraw, representing one of several simultaneous crises straining imperial resources during the Italian campaigns.

places.name: Princes' Islands
places.summary: A chain of islands in the Sea of Marmara that Mehmed stormed during the siege, killing the garrison and selling the inhabitants into slavery as a demonstration to Constantinople of the price of defiance.

summaries_by_episode.16: A chain of islands in the Sea of Marmara that Mehmed stormed during the siege, killing the garrison and selling the inhabitants into slavery as a demonstration to Constantinople of the price of defiance.

places.name: Ravenna
places.summary: Ravenna was the capital of the Ostrogothic kingdom and Vittigis's stronghold. Brownworth notes it was 'virtually impregnable,' which is why Vittigis offered Belisarius the western imperial crown in exchange for sparing the city. Belisarius instead used the offer as a ruse to capture it and ship Vittigis to Constantinople.

summaries_by_episode.2: The city Diocletian traveled to in 304 to escape the marshy climate of Rome after falling ill at the celebration of his twentieth year in power, collapsing on the road before eventually recovering.
summaries_by_episode.3: The city to which Severus fled after his army was bribed away from him at the walls of Rome. He was captured there, forced to abdicate, and killed.
summaries_by_episode.6: The capital of the Western Empire at the time of its fall, where Odoacer entered in September 476 and showed clemency to Romulus Augustulus. It was also where Theodoric blockaded Odoacer before the final banquet murder.
summaries_by_episode.8: Ravenna was the capital of the Ostrogothic kingdom and Vittigis's stronghold. Brownworth notes it was 'virtually impregnable,' which is why Vittigis offered Belisarius the western imperial crown in exchange for sparing the city. Belisarius instead used the offer as a ruse to capture it and ship Vittigis to Constantinople.
summaries_by_episode.9: One of the four Italian cities still defying Totila in 542, and the site where Narses paused to distribute back pay and gather more troops before marching on Rome. Brownworth also notes there are famous mosaics of Justinian and Theodora in Ravenna where the modern visitor can come face to face with the couple.
summaries_by_episode.11: The last imperial stronghold in Italy, taken by the Lombard king in 751. Its fall prompted the pope to seek the protection of the Franks rather than the iconoclast emperor in Constantinople.

places.summary_synthesized: Located on the northeastern coast of Italy, Ravenna served at various points as the capital of the Western Roman Empire, the Ostrogothic kingdom, and the last Byzantine stronghold in Italy. Its defensible position made it a recurring refuge and stronghold: emperors and generals retreated to it, Odoacer entered it in 476 after deposing the last western emperor, and Belisarius captured it through ruse in the Gothic War after its Ostrogothic defenders considered it virtually impregnable. Its fall to the Lombards in 751 ended Byzantine control of the city and pushed the papacy to seek Frankish protection rather than rely on Constantinople.

places.name: Rhodes
places.summary: The island famous as the proud possessor of the Colossus, one of the seven wonders of the ancient world, which had fallen during an earthquake 900 years before the Arab conquest. After taking the island, the Arab commander broke up the statue and sold it for scrap, requiring 900 camels to haul away the pieces. Rhodes was also the place of exile for Martina, widow of Heraclius.

summaries_by_episode.11: The island famous as the proud possessor of the Colossus, one of the seven wonders of the ancient world, which had fallen during an earthquake 900 years before the Arab conquest. After taking the island, the Arab commander broke up the statue and sold it for scrap, requiring 900 camels to haul away the pieces. Rhodes was also the place of exile for Martina, widow of Heraclius.

places.name: Rimini
places.summary: Rimini was the town captured by John's 2,000 cavalry, 200 miles behind Gothic lines and only 33 miles from the Gothic capital Ravenna. When John refused to withdraw and Vittigis sent a relief force, the fall of Rimini threatened to become a decisive reversal; Narses insisted it be rescued and Belisarius did so with a brilliant amphibious deception.

summaries_by_episode.8: Rimini was the town captured by John's 2,000 cavalry, 200 miles behind Gothic lines and only 33 miles from the Gothic capital Ravenna. When John refused to withdraw and Vittigis sent a relief force, the fall of Rimini threatened to become a decisive reversal; Narses insisted it be rescued and Belisarius did so with a brilliant amphibious deception.

places.name: River Margus
places.summary: The site of the decisive battle between Diocletian and Carinus near modern-day Belgrade. Carinus had the larger army and was winning until one of his own officers, whose wife he had seduced, struck him down, handing victory to Diocletian.

summaries_by_episode.2: The site of the decisive battle between Diocletian and Carinus near modern-day Belgrade. Carinus had the larger army and was winning until one of his own officers, whose wife he had seduced, struck him down, handing victory to Diocletian.

places.name: Rome
places.summary: Though of declining importance as a capital, Rome still held certain hereditary rights including an exemption from all taxes. Maxentius seized it, declared it tax-free, and held it as his power base, defended by bribes rather than an army. Constantine entered it triumphantly after the Battle of the Milvian Bridge, and it was here that his triumphal arch, still standing just outside the Colosseum, made careful non-Christian reference to divinity.

summaries_by_episode.1: Rome is the original imperial capital and the yardstick against which Byzantine identity is measured throughout this episode. Brownworth notes that the empire later called Byzantine never controlled Rome for most of its history, which is precisely why historians felt the need to give it a different name; he also discusses how Rome itself changed enormously between the age of Brutus and the age of Augustus.
summaries_by_episode.2: The historic capital of the Roman Empire, though Brownworth notes Diocletian deliberately shifted the emphasis of imperial government away from the city to wherever the emperor resided. Diocletian visited Rome for the first time in his entire reign only in 304, to unveil his famous baths, before falling deathly ill.
summaries_by_episode.3: Though of declining importance as a capital, Rome still held certain hereditary rights including an exemption from all taxes. Maxentius seized it, declared it tax-free, and held it as his power base, defended by bribes rather than an army. Constantine entered it triumphantly after the Battle of the Milvian Bridge, and it was here that his triumphal arch, still standing just outside the Colosseum, made careful non-Christian reference to divinity.
summaries_by_episode.4: The Eternal City that Constantine had not entered since his victory at the Milvian Bridge thirteen years earlier. His visit in January 326 ended disastrously, with mutual contempt between emperor and city; after consecrating the new Basilica of St. Peter's he left and never returned, accelerating his decision to build a new capital in the East.
summaries_by_episode.5: Mentioned in the epilogue as a symbol of the empire's decline after Julian — the city of Rome itself was sacked in less than a century after his death.
summaries_by_episode.6: The ancient capital of the Western Empire, sacked by the Vandals for 14 days in 455, stripped of all wealth including gilded copper roofs. The sack and eventual abandonment of Rome as a capital led the populace to turn to the pope for both spiritual and temporal authority, ushering in the medieval papacy.
summaries_by_episode.8: Rome was the great symbolic prize of the Italian campaign — embarrassingly un-Roman as long as it was outside imperial control. Belisarius entered it in December after getting the pope's endorsement, then held it for a year and nine days against Vittigis's Gothic siege, improvising flour mills on the city's rivers after the aqueducts were cut.
summaries_by_episode.9: Rome changed hands multiple times in the episode — defended unsuccessfully by the corrupt Bessus, then lost when the garrison betrayed the city and Totila took possession of an almost deserted ruin of 500 people; Belisarius then swept in and repaired it; Totila recaptured it again; and finally Narses restored it to Byzantine control for the fifth time in the war.
summaries_by_episode.11: The city where Pope Leo III was ambushed and beaten, and where Charlemagne came to testify on the pope's behalf in 800. It was here, at Christmas Mass, that Pope Leo III crowned Charlemagne Holy Roman Emperor.
summaries_by_episode.12: The seat of the papacy and the western rival to Constantinople's ecclesiastical authority. Photius challenged Rome's claim to universal spiritual authority, and Basil later played the pope against himself over Bulgaria before recalling Photius.
summaries_by_episode.14: The subject of a brutal three-day sack by Robert Giscard in which he set fire to the city and gutted most of its ancient buildings — carried out while Robert was returning to Italy to suppress the Alexios-backed Norman revolt and drive out the German Emperor.
summaries_by_episode.16: The destination of John VIII's desperate embassy seeking a crusade against the Turks. Brownworth notes John found Europe still asleep to the danger and ultimately promised to convert the empire to Catholicism — signing the decree of union there — in a failed bid for military aid.

places.summary_synthesized: The original capital of the Roman Empire, Rome gradually lost political primacy as successive emperors governed from wherever they resided, yet it retained powerful symbolic weight and hereditary privileges throughout late antiquity and the medieval period. It changed hands repeatedly—sacked by Vandals in 455, contested five times during the Byzantine-Gothic wars, seized and lost by figures from Maxentius to Totila, and later crowned Charlemagne as Holy Roman Emperor in 800—each episode reshaping its political and religious character. Over time the city's secular power eroded while papal authority expanded to fill the vacuum, making Rome the seat of a western ecclesiastical rival to Constantinople whose claims to universal spiritual jurisdiction remained a persistent source of conflict with the Byzantine east.

places.name: Saint Petersburg
places.summary: Brownworth notes that Peter the Great tried to copy Byzantine domes and spires when building St. Petersburg, making the city a testament to the lasting architectural and cultural influence of Byzantium on Russian civilization.

summaries_by_episode.17: Brownworth notes that Peter the Great tried to copy Byzantine domes and spires when building St. Petersburg, making the city a testament to the lasting architectural and cultural influence of Byzantium on Russian civilization.

places.name: Samosata
places.summary: An important fortress captured by Basil's brother during the decade of slow but steady advances against the Saracens.

summaries_by_episode.12: An important fortress captured by Basil's brother during the decade of slow but steady advances against the Saracens.

places.name: Sardinia
places.summary: Sardinia is mentioned as the destination of a large Vandal army that Justinian had encouraged to revolt, unknowingly stripping Carthage's defenses just as Belisarius was sailing to invade. The revolt drew away enough forces that Belisarius could land and march to Carthage unopposed.

summaries_by_episode.8: Sardinia is mentioned as the destination of a large Vandal army that Justinian had encouraged to revolt, unknowingly stripping Carthage's defenses just as Belisarius was sailing to invade. The revolt drew away enough forces that Belisarius could land and march to Carthage unopposed.

places.name: Saxa Rubra
places.summary: The site of the main engagement before the Battle of the Milvian Bridge, described as 'Red Rocks,' located seven or eight miles northeast of Rome. It was here that Constantine had his famous vision and routed Maxentius's forces, chasing them back to the Milvian Bridge.

summaries_by_episode.3: The site of the main engagement before the Battle of the Milvian Bridge, described as 'Red Rocks,' located seven or eight miles northeast of Rome. It was here that Constantine had his famous vision and routed Maxentius's forces, chasing them back to the Milvian Bridge.

places.name: Sea of Marmara
places.summary: One of the two bodies of water anchoring the Theodosian land walls, mentioned as Brownworth describes the astonishing defensive perimeter held by seven thousand Byzantine defenders in the final siege.

summaries_by_episode.16: The inland sea where Mehmed stormed the Princes' Islands during the siege, killing the garrison and selling the inhabitants into slavery as an act of intimidation toward Constantinople's defenders.
summaries_by_episode.17: One of the two bodies of water anchoring the Theodosian land walls, mentioned as Brownworth describes the astonishing defensive perimeter held by seven thousand Byzantine defenders in the final siege.

places.summary_synthesized: The Sea of Marmara is an inland sea flanking Constantinople to the south, forming one of the two bodies of water that anchored the city's Theodosian land walls and made its defensive perimeter formidable even for a garrison of only seven thousand. During the Ottoman siege, Mehmed used it as a theater of intimidation, storming the Princes' Islands within its waters, killing the garrisons, and selling the inhabitants into slavery to demonstrate his intentions to Constantinople's defenders.

places.name: Seleucia
places.summary: The coastal plain in Asia Minor where Frederick Barbarossa drowned in the Chalycadnus River in the heat of summer, having crossed the mountains onto the plain and ridden ahead of his men to swim. His death there immediately caused his immense army to disintegrate.

summaries_by_episode.15: The coastal plain in Asia Minor where Frederick Barbarossa drowned in the Chalycadnus River in the heat of summer, having crossed the mountains onto the plain and ridden ahead of his men to swim. His death there immediately caused his immense army to disintegrate.

places.name: Sicily
places.summary: Constans II moved the imperial capital here to better protect Italy, an unorthodox decision that was reversed after his assassination. Sicily also features as the proposed place of exile for Irene in the conspiracy hatched by senior army officers, and later as the site of a governor who declared independence and placed himself under Saracen protection after Irene's purge of the army.

summaries_by_episode.8: Sicily served as a resupply and intelligence-gathering stop for Belisarius on his way to North Africa, where Procopius obtained crucial information about the Vandals' unpreparedness. It was also the first territory conquered in the Italian campaign, taken with barely an effort.
summaries_by_episode.9: The island where Justinian's incompetent overall commander sailed and then refused to leave, even as Naples was being starved into submission — used by Brownworth to illustrate the paralysis of Byzantine command in Italy.
summaries_by_episode.11: Constans II moved the imperial capital here to better protect Italy, an unorthodox decision that was reversed after his assassination. Sicily also features as the proposed place of exile for Irene in the conspiracy hatched by senior army officers, and later as the site of a governor who declared independence and placed himself under Saracen protection after Irene's purge of the army.
summaries_by_episode.12: One of the western territories Bardas failed to hold against Saracen invasion. Basil also failed to defend it, and its last imperial stronghold of Syracuse fell in 878.
summaries_by_episode.13: Mentioned at the end as the island reconquered by Belisarius in the 6th century and then lost to the Muslims in the 9th — the target of the invasion Basil II was planning when he fell ill and died on December 15, 1025.
summaries_by_episode.14: Most of the island fell to Robert Giscard by 1072, completing his conquest of the south after taking Bari. Brownworth uses Sicily's fall alongside Bari's as evidence that by this point the Normans were without peer in brute strength and fighting ability.
summaries_by_episode.15: The most common destination for Byzantines fleeing Andronicus's reign of terror, whose Greek court welcomed them and whose king William the Good used them—and a false Alexius II—as justification for a massive invasion of the Byzantine Empire.

places.summary_synthesized: A large Mediterranean island lying between North Africa and the Italian peninsula, Sicily occupied a strategically significant position throughout Byzantine history. It served Belisarius as a resupply and intelligence base during his sixth-century campaigns, was briefly used as an imperial capital by Constans II, and changed hands repeatedly over the centuries—falling to Muslim forces when Syracuse was lost in 878, and ultimately to the Normans by 1072. The island remained a point of contention into the eleventh and twelfth centuries, figuring in Basil II's unrealized reconquest plans and later sheltering Byzantine refugees from Andronicus I's reign of terror, whose Norman rulers used them as a pretext for invading the empire.

places.name: Sirmium
places.summary: The city where the imperial party stopped in February 326 on the way to Rome, and where Constantine suddenly launched into a frenzy of killing — arresting and executing his son Crispus and a half-brother, then murdering his wife Fausta.

summaries_by_episode.4: The city where the imperial party stopped in February 326 on the way to Rome, and where Constantine suddenly launched into a frenzy of killing — arresting and executing his son Crispus and a half-brother, then murdering his wife Fausta.

places.name: Sofia (Serdica)
places.summary: The city to which Constantine moved his capital eastward, described by Brownworth as a 'curious choice' for an emperor whose domain stretched to the Atlantic, and justifiable only if Constantine viewed Licinius as a greater threat than any barbarian or western usurper.

summaries_by_episode.3: The city to which Constantine moved his capital eastward, described by Brownworth as a 'curious choice' for an emperor whose domain stretched to the Atlantic, and justifiable only if Constantine viewed Licinius as a greater threat than any barbarian or western usurper.

places.name: Southern Italy
places.summary: The theater of Norman expansion and the former seat of Byzantine power in Italy. The Normans first arrived here as mercenaries, then captured key fortresses and spread throughout the Italian peninsula. Alexios exploited Norman resentment here by bribing barons to revolt against Robert Giscard, forcing his recall from Greece.

summaries_by_episode.14: The theater of Norman expansion and the former seat of Byzantine power in Italy. The Normans first arrived here as mercenaries, then captured key fortresses and spread throughout the Italian peninsula. Alexios exploited Norman resentment here by bribing barons to revolt against Robert Giscard, forcing his recall from Greece.

places.name: Spain
places.summary: Two emissaries from an orthodox faction in Spain appealed to Justinian for help against their Arian king. With only a few hundred men and the octogenarian general Liberius, Justinian seized the opportunity and conquered about an eighth of Spain — Brownworth marveling that almost any other ruler would have dismissed the idea as foolhardy.

summaries_by_episode.4: Part of the long route Islamic armies were forced to take around the Constantinople walls and through North Africa before reaching Europe, where they were eventually stopped at the Battle of Tours.
summaries_by_episode.9: Two emissaries from an orthodox faction in Spain appealed to Justinian for help against their Arian king. With only a few hundred men and the octogenarian general Liberius, Justinian seized the opportunity and conquered about an eighth of Spain — Brownworth marveling that almost any other ruler would have dismissed the idea as foolhardy.
summaries_by_episode.10: One of Justinian's reconquests, Spain was described as 'virtually ignored' after Justinian's death, with the Visigoths poised to swallow what remained of imperial Spain under Phocas.
summaries_by_episode.17: Named alongside North Africa as the roundabout route Islamic armies were forced to take because Constantinople's walls held, with Brownworth arguing this overextension of Muslim forces gave Europe the time needed to resist conquest.

places.summary_synthesized: Located on the western edge of Europe, Spain became a minor Byzantine territory in the sixth century when Justinian exploited a religious dispute to seize roughly an eighth of the peninsula, deploying the elderly general Liberius with a small force to support an orthodox faction against its Arian Visigothic king. The foothold proved short-lived, as the territory was largely neglected after Justinian's death and the Visigoths moved steadily to reclaim it. Spain later figured in the broader strategic significance of Constantinople's walls, which forced Islamic armies to take the long overland route through North Africa and into the peninsula, a costly overextension that ultimately contributed to the Muslim advance being halted at the Battle of Tours.

places.name: Spalatum (Diocletian's Palace / Split)
places.summary: The site of Diocletian's retirement palace, so large it later became a city — the modern city of Split in Croatia. His octagonal mausoleum, Brownworth notes, remains the heart of the city.

summaries_by_episode.2: The site of Diocletian's retirement palace, so large it later became a city — the modern city of Split in Croatia. His octagonal mausoleum, Brownworth notes, remains the heart of the city.

places.name: Sparta
places.summary: One of several sources of precious stone used in the construction of the Hagia Sophia, mentioned alongside Egypt, Libya, and North Africa to illustrate the empire-wide scope of the building project.

summaries_by_episode.9: One of several sources of precious stone used in the construction of the Hagia Sophia, mentioned alongside Egypt, Libya, and North Africa to illustrate the empire-wide scope of the building project.

places.name: St. Mark's Basilica
places.summary: Brownworth directs the listener to look for the porphyry sculpture of the four tetrarchs in the southeast corner of the exterior. The sculpture was stolen from Constantinople during the Fourth Crusade and incorporated into the church's masonry.

summaries_by_episode.2: Brownworth directs the listener to look for the porphyry sculpture of the four tetrarchs in the southeast corner of the exterior. The sculpture was stolen from Constantinople during the Fourth Crusade and incorporated into the church's masonry.

places.name: St. Mark's Basilica
places.summary: Brownworth cites St. Mark's in Venice as patterned after the vanished Church of the Holy Apostles in Constantinople, presenting it as one of the most direct examples of Byzantine architectural influence on Western sacred buildings.

summaries_by_episode.17: Brownworth cites St. Mark's in Venice as patterned after the vanished Church of the Holy Apostles in Constantinople, presenting it as one of the most direct examples of Byzantine architectural influence on Western sacred buildings.

places.name: Syracuse
places.summary: The last imperial stronghold in Sicily, it fell to the Saracens in 878, marking the end of Byzantine rule on the island.

summaries_by_episode.12: The last imperial stronghold in Sicily, it fell to the Saracens in 878, marking the end of Byzantine rule on the island.

places.name: Syria
places.summary: Reconquered by the first usurping general before Romanus II, and then again by John Tzimiskes during his eastern campaigns. Also mentioned as the region twice invaded by the Fatimid Caliphate during Basil II's Bulgarian campaigns, drawing the emperor east at speed.

summaries_by_episode.6: The province where a rebel was trying to restore paganism, prompting Zeno to send Illus to suppress the uprising. A violent quarrel there between Illus and Zeno's incompetent brother led Zeno to declare Illus a public enemy, driving him into the rebel camp.
summaries_by_episode.10: The region into which Muslim forces poured after the Christological controversy undermined Byzantine unity, conquering Damascus and besieging Jerusalem. The Byzantine army of 80,000 retreated there to pursue the Muslims, who fell back into the Syrian desert.
summaries_by_episode.13: Reconquered by the first usurping general before Romanus II, and then again by John Tzimiskes during his eastern campaigns. Also mentioned as the region twice invaded by the Fatimid Caliphate during Basil II's Bulgarian campaigns, drawing the emperor east at speed.

places.summary_synthesized: A province on the eastern frontier of the Byzantine Empire, Syria was a recurring site of military and political crisis across several centuries. In the late fifth century it hosted a pagan-linked rebellion that drew the general Illus into open conflict with Emperor Zeno; in the seventh century, Christological divisions within the empire weakened resistance there, allowing Muslim forces to take Damascus and press toward Jerusalem. It was subsequently reconquered during the tenth-century eastern campaigns and twice invaded by the Fatimid Caliphate during Basil II's Bulgarian wars, forcing the emperor to redirect his attention eastward on each occasion.

places.name: Tarsus
places.summary: Listed as one of the major cities that fell to Nikephoros Phokas during his eastern campaign, along with Cyprus, Aleppo, and the rest of Mesopotamia and Syria, before his crowning achievement of recapturing Antioch.

summaries_by_episode.13: Listed as one of the major cities that fell to Nikephoros Phokas during his eastern campaign, along with Cyprus, Aleppo, and the rest of Mesopotamia and Syria, before his crowning achievement of recapturing Antioch.

places.name: Tefrike
places.summary: The main stronghold of the Paulician heretics, captured by Basil during his personal campaign through Asia Minor. With its fall, the Paulician threat to the empire was effectively neutralized.

summaries_by_episode.12: The main stronghold of the Paulician heretics, captured by Basil during his personal campaign through Asia Minor. With its fall, the Paulician threat to the empire was effectively neutralized.

places.name: Temple of Apollo (Antioch)
places.summary: Gallus had buried a Christian martyr in the precinct of this temple. When Julian had the body exhumed, massive riots broke out. The entire complex later burned, and Julian blamed the Christians and closed the city's cathedral.

summaries_by_episode.5: Gallus had buried a Christian martyr in the precinct of this temple. When Julian had the body exhumed, massive riots broke out. The entire complex later burned, and Julian blamed the Christians and closed the city's cathedral.

places.name: Temple of the Sun
places.summary: The source of eight columns incorporated into the Hagia Sophia, used by Brownworth to illustrate the scale of Justinian's ambition in drawing building materials from the most venerable monuments of the old Roman world.

summaries_by_episode.9: The source of eight columns incorporated into the Hagia Sophia, used by Brownworth to illustrate the scale of Justinian's ambition in drawing building materials from the most venerable monuments of the old Roman world.

places.name: The Alps
places.summary: The mountain range Constantine crossed in 312 when he invaded Italy to seize Maxentius's territory, the crossing being a major military undertaking that led to the sack of Turin and Verona and ultimately the Battle of the Milvian Bridge.

summaries_by_episode.3: The mountain range Constantine crossed in 312 when he invaded Italy to seize Maxentius's territory, the crossing being a major military undertaking that led to the sack of Turin and Verona and ultimately the Battle of the Milvian Bridge.

places.name: The Balkans
places.summary: A persistent source of trouble for Alexios throughout his reign. At his accession the Balkans were once again in revolt, threatening from the west alongside the Normans. Later a Cuman pretender inspired a popular revolt there, which Alexios solved by bribing disgruntled Cumans to assassinate the pretender, whereupon the revolt simply melted away.

summaries_by_episode.3: The region heavily granted to Constantine under the treaty with Licinius after the 316 campaign, following Valens's fight to a standstill in Thrace.
summaries_by_episode.10: Repeatedly stripped of troops to fight on other fronts, the Balkans were invaded by Slavs and Avars and described as essentially gone from Byzantine control by Heraclius' coronation. During the crisis of 626, the Avar khaganate united virtually every barbarian tribe in the Balkans and moved them against Constantinople.
summaries_by_episode.14: A persistent source of trouble for Alexios throughout his reign. At his accession the Balkans were once again in revolt, threatening from the west alongside the Normans. Later a Cuman pretender inspired a popular revolt there, which Alexios solved by bribing disgruntled Cumans to assassinate the pretender, whereupon the revolt simply melted away.
summaries_by_episode.15: The Sicilian army swept across the Balkans within two months and arrived before Thessalonica. The region was also plagued by the Bulgarian revolt and subsequent Byzantine military failures under Isaac II.

places.summary_synthesized: A broad region stretching across southeastern Europe, the Balkans were a recurring flashpoint throughout Byzantine history, passing in and out of imperial control across the centuries. Repeatedly stripped of defensive forces to meet crises elsewhere, the region fell to Slavic and Avar incursions by the early seventh century, and in 626 the Avar khaganate assembled a coalition of virtually every Balkan tribe for a direct assault on Constantinople. The area remained a persistent source of instability into the later imperial period, troubled by Norman pressure, Cuman-inspired revolts, Bulgarian uprisings, and foreign armies sweeping through on their way to major Byzantine cities.

places.name: Theodosian Walls
places.summary: Brownworth describes these walls as the most formidable in the ancient or medieval worlds — over a thousand years old at the time of the siege — consisting of a 60-foot moat, an outer wall 30 feet high with 96 towers, and an inner wall 40 feet higher with 96 more towers. Even Attila the Hun turned away from them, but Orban's great cannon reduced them to rubble.

summaries_by_episode.16: Brownworth describes these walls as the most formidable in the ancient or medieval worlds — over a thousand years old at the time of the siege — consisting of a 60-foot moat, an outer wall 30 feet high with 96 towers, and an inner wall 40 feet higher with 96 more towers. Even Attila the Hun turned away from them, but Orban's great cannon reduced them to rubble.
summaries_by_episode.17: Brownworth marvels that the Theodosian land walls, which still march from the Sea of Marmara to the Golden Horn, were held by just seven thousand men for over a month against more than ten times their number, calling it one of history's great feats of resistance.

places.summary_synthesized: A massive triple-line fortification running from the Sea of Marmara to the Golden Horn, the Theodosian Walls consisted of a 60-foot moat, an outer wall 30 feet high with 96 towers, and an inner wall standing 40 feet taller still, with 96 additional towers. Standing for over a thousand years, they repelled even Attila the Hun and were considered the most formidable defensive works of the ancient or medieval world. In 1453 a garrison of just seven thousand defenders held them against more than ten times their number for over a month before Ottoman artillery, including Orban's great cannon, finally reduced them to rubble.

places.name: Thessalonica
places.summary: The second city of the Byzantine Empire, whose fall to the Sicilian invasion under William II highlighted the catastrophic failures of Andronicus's regime. The incompetent governor had made no preparations: he ran out of arrows, catapult stones, and even water, and after just nine days German mercenaries were bribed to open the gates to the brutal Sicilian sack.

summaries_by_episode.13: Basil II's first stop before entering Bulgaria on his great campaign was Thessalonica, where he tracked down a monk who had been present at his baptism and obtained his blessing and a promise to pray for him every night for the duration — a campaign that would last more than three decades.
summaries_by_episode.15: The second city of the Byzantine Empire, whose fall to the Sicilian invasion under William II highlighted the catastrophic failures of Andronicus's regime. The incompetent governor had made no preparations: he ran out of arrows, catapult stones, and even water, and after just nine days German mercenaries were bribed to open the gates to the brutal Sicilian sack.

places.summary_synthesized: The second city of the Byzantine Empire, Thessalonica served as a major strategic and symbolic center throughout Byzantine history. Basil II stopped there at the outset of his decades-long Bulgarian campaign to seek the blessing of a monk who had witnessed his baptism. In 1185 the city fell to a Sicilian invasion under William II after just nine days, its governor having made no preparations for the siege — a catastrophic failure that underscored the broader collapse of Andronicus's regime.

places.name: Thrace
places.summary: The Byzantine region invaded by Tsar Samuel during Basil II's early reign, with Samuel selling the population of its major city into slavery and carrying off the bones of its major saint to Bulgaria's capital cathedral — an insult that forced Basil to march on Bulgaria for the first time.

summaries_by_episode.3: The region where Valens, Licinius's newly appointed co-emperor, managed to fight Constantine to a standstill after Constantine's invasion of Licinius's territory in 316.
summaries_by_episode.13: The Byzantine region invaded by Tsar Samuel during Basil II's early reign, with Samuel selling the population of its major city into slavery and carrying off the bones of its major saint to Bulgaria's capital cathedral — an insult that forced Basil to march on Bulgaria for the first time.
summaries_by_episode.15: The region in which Frederick Barbarossa turned his army loose after Isaac II's provocations, occupying Adrianople. Bulgaria also ravaged as far as Macedonian Thrace during Alexius III's neglectful reign.

places.modern_name: Thrace (parts of modern Greece, Bulgaria, Turkey)
places.summary_synthesized: A region in the southeastern Balkans, Thrace served as contested ground across several centuries of Byzantine history. It was the theater where Valens fought Constantine to a standstill in 316, later suffered Tsar Samuel's devastating raids during Basil II's early reign, and endured further devastation under Frederick Barbarossa, whose forces occupied Adrianople in retaliation for Byzantine provocations. Bulgarian incursions extended as far as Macedonian Thrace during the neglectful reign of Alexius III, underscoring the region's persistent vulnerability to outside powers.

places.name: Throat Cutter (Rumeli Hisarı)
places.summary: The fortress built by Mehmed II on the European side of the Bosphorus in just four and a half months — a feat Brownworth credits to magnificent Turkish organization and efficiency. Named the 'Throat Cutter,' it allowed Mehmed to control all Bosphorus traffic and cut off Constantinople's food supply, and is where Orban's cannon was initially mounted.

summaries_by_episode.16: The fortress built by Mehmed II on the European side of the Bosphorus in just four and a half months — a feat Brownworth credits to magnificent Turkish organization and efficiency. Named the 'Throat Cutter,' it allowed Mehmed to control all Bosphorus traffic and cut off Constantinople's food supply, and is where Orban's cannon was initially mounted.

places.name: Tiber River
places.summary: The river crossed by the Milvian Bridge, into which half of Maxentius's army was cast when the pontoon bridge collapsed and the old Milvian Bridge became a death trap for the panicked soldiers. Maxentius's corpse was found washed up on its shore.

summaries_by_episode.3: The river crossed by the Milvian Bridge, into which half of Maxentius's army was cast when the pontoon bridge collapsed and the old Milvian Bridge became a death trap for the panicked soldiers. Maxentius's corpse was found washed up on its shore.
summaries_by_episode.6: The river into which the dismembered pieces of the unnamed elderly senator-emperor were thrown by the Roman populace who blamed him for allowing the Vandals to sack the city.
summaries_by_episode.9: The river along which Belisarius launched his bold amphibious relief of Rome — half his men marching on the southern bank and half sailing upstream — before he was turned back by the false news of Isaac's capture.

places.summary_synthesized: A river running through Rome that recurs across pivotal moments in the city's late antique history. It swallowed half of Maxentius's army when the pontoon bridge collapsed during the Battle of Milvian Bridge in 312, and his body was later found on its banks; over a century later, an emperor's dismembered remains were cast into it by a Roman populace enraged by his failure to prevent the Vandal sack of the city. In the sixth century, Belisarius used the Tiber as an axis of advance during his relief of Rome, sending half his force upstream by boat before the operation was abandoned.

places.name: Tigris River
places.summary: The river on whose banks the emperor Carus was killed by a bolt of lightning in 283, halting the Roman advance into Persia. Brownworth implies the lightning story was cover for assassination by Diocletian.

summaries_by_episode.2: The river on whose banks the emperor Carus was killed by a bolt of lightning in 283, halting the Roman advance into Persia. Brownworth implies the lightning story was cover for assassination by Diocletian.
summaries_by_episode.5: Julian recrossed the Tigris after abandoning the siege of Ctesiphon, beginning his ill-fated retreat northward through Persian territory while constantly harassed by the enemy.

places.summary_synthesized: A major river forming part of the boundary between Roman and Persian territory, the Tigris marked the eastern limit of Roman military ambition on multiple occasions. The emperor Carus died on its banks in 283, allegedly struck by lightning—a story some sources treat as cover for foul play—halting a Roman advance into Persia. Over a century later, Julian crossed back over the river after abandoning his siege of Ctesiphon, beginning a harassed retreat northward that would prove fatal to his campaign.

places.name: Trajan's Gate
places.summary: A narrow mountain pass in Bulgaria where Samuel set a devastating ambush for Basil II's first Bulgarian campaign, nearly wiping out the Byzantine army and deeply humiliating the emperor. The defeat taught Basil to never again march through narrow passes without securing the flanks, and his vow of revenge would define thirty years of subsequent warfare.

summaries_by_episode.13: A narrow mountain pass in Bulgaria where Samuel set a devastating ambush for Basil II's first Bulgarian campaign, nearly wiping out the Byzantine army and deeply humiliating the emperor. The defeat taught Basil to never again march through narrow passes without securing the flanks, and his vow of revenge would define thirty years of subsequent warfare.

places.name: Trebizond
places.summary: The last surviving Byzantine successor state, which Brownworth notes held out for seven years after the fall of Constantinople before finally falling on 15 August 1461, marking the true end of the last Byzantine throne.

summaries_by_episode.16: One of two imperial offshoots Brownworth mentions as remaining stubbornly independent after the Latin capture of Constantinople, sapping the already weakened strength of the empire instead of reuniting with it.
summaries_by_episode.17: The last surviving Byzantine successor state, which Brownworth notes held out for seven years after the fall of Constantinople before finally falling on 15 August 1461, marking the true end of the last Byzantine throne.

places.summary_synthesized: A city on the southern Black Sea coast, Trebizond became one of the Byzantine successor states that emerged after the Latin capture of Constantinople in 1204, remaining stubbornly independent rather than reuniting with the restored empire. It outlasted all other Byzantine successor states, holding out for seven years after the fall of Constantinople in 1453. Its final surrender on 15 August 1461 marked the extinction of the last Byzantine throne.

places.name: Trier
places.summary: The western city to which Athanasius was banished after being deposed in absentia when he refused to reinstate Arius and fled the pro-Arian council summoned by Constantine.

summaries_by_episode.4: The western city to which Athanasius was banished after being deposed in absentia when he refused to reinstate Arius and fled the pro-Arian council summoned by Constantine.

places.name: Tripoli
places.summary: The one major coastal city that John Tzimiskes notably did not capture during his final eastern campaign, mentioned in passing as the sole exception to an otherwise total sweep of the Levantine coast.

summaries_by_episode.13: The one major coastal city that John Tzimiskes notably did not capture during his final eastern campaign, mentioned in passing as the sole exception to an otherwise total sweep of the Levantine coast.

places.name: Troy
places.summary: Mentioned as the city from which Aeneas allegedly brought the statue of Athena that Constantine later placed in the base of his porphyry column in Constantinople.

summaries_by_episode.4: Mentioned as the city from which Aeneas allegedly brought the statue of Athena that Constantine later placed in the base of his porphyry column in Constantinople.

places.name: Turin
places.summary: One of the Italian cities sacked by Constantine during his invasion of Italy in 312 as he advanced on Rome.

summaries_by_episode.3: One of the Italian cities sacked by Constantine during his invasion of Italy in 312 as he advanced on Rome.

places.name: Venice
places.summary: The Venetian Republic, whose expansive trading rights within the Byzantine Empire—granted by Alexius I and built upon despite attempts to curtail them—gave it a virtual stranglehold on Byzantine commerce. Manuel I's arrest and property confiscation of all Venetians earned dangerous animosity. Venice later became the driving force behind the diversion of the Fourth Crusade to Constantinople, with the Doge Enrico Dandolo using the crusaders' debt as leverage.

summaries_by_episode.11: The horrified citizens of the Venetian lagoon rebelled against Leo III's iconoclast edict and appointed a local leader as Doge, giving birth to the Venetian Republic — described by Brownworth as both an ally and inveterate enemy of the Byzantine Empire.
summaries_by_episode.13: Basil II invited Venice to invade Dalmatia in the empire's name, solving two problems: protecting Byzantine cities on the Dalmatian coast and forcing Samuel into a two-front war. The Venetians were eager to use the forests there for timber for their navy.
summaries_by_episode.15: The Venetian Republic, whose expansive trading rights within the Byzantine Empire—granted by Alexius I and built upon despite attempts to curtail them—gave it a virtual stranglehold on Byzantine commerce. Manuel I's arrest and property confiscation of all Venetians earned dangerous animosity. Venice later became the driving force behind the diversion of the Fourth Crusade to Constantinople, with the Doge Enrico Dandolo using the crusaders' debt as leverage.
summaries_by_episode.16: Brownworth mentions Venice in multiple unflattering contexts: the real Byzantine crown jewels were in pawn to Venice by 1347; Venice promised 15 galleys and 800 men but delayed departure until April 20 and the fleet never arrived; and several Venetian merchants snuck out of the besieged city taking 700 needed men with them, though the Venetian merchants who stayed helped defend the harbor.
summaries_by_episode.17: Brownworth notes that St. Mark's Basilica in Venice is patterned after the vanished Church of the Holy Apostles in Constantinople, making Venice a physical repository of Byzantine architectural legacy in the heart of Western Europe.

places.summary_synthesized: A city-state founded in the Venetian lagoon following a rebellion against Byzantine iconoclasm in the eighth century, Venice developed an enduring and deeply ambivalent relationship with the empire—sometimes its military partner, ultimately its commercial and political predator. Trading privileges granted by Alexius I gave the republic a stranglehold on Byzantine commerce, and accumulated grievances culminated in Venice's orchestration of the Fourth Crusade's diversion to Constantinople in 1204. The city's physical landscape preserves traces of that entangled history: St. Mark's Basilica, modeled on Constantinople's Church of the Holy Apostles, stands as an architectural echo of the empire Venice helped destroy.

places.name: Verona
places.summary: One of the Italian cities sacked by Constantine during his advance on Rome in 312.

summaries_by_episode.3: One of the Italian cities sacked by Constantine during his advance on Rome in 312.

places.name: Western Europe
places.summary: Brownworth frames Western Europe as the primary beneficiary and principal ingrate of Byzantine civilization: Byzantine scholars fleeing Constantinople brought classical learning to an awakening West that then quickly forgot where that learning came from, leading to the Renaissance while dismissing its Byzantine source.

summaries_by_episode.17: Brownworth frames Western Europe as the primary beneficiary and principal ingrate of Byzantine civilization: Byzantine scholars fleeing Constantinople brought classical learning to an awakening West that then quickly forgot where that learning came from, leading to the Renaissance while dismissing its Byzantine source.

places.name: York
places.summary: The Roman city of Eboracum in Britain where Constantius Chlorus died the spring after his campaign against the Picts. It was here that Constantine's father's troops, who had instantly admired the young Constantine, hailed him as emperor.

summaries_by_episode.3: The Roman city of Eboracum in Britain where Constantius Chlorus died the spring after his campaign against the Picts. It was here that Constantine's father's troops, who had instantly admired the young Constantine, hailed him as emperor.

places.name: Zara
places.summary: The city on the Dalmatian coast that Venice depended on for timber but which had come under the sway of the Christian King of Hungary. Dandolo offered to forgive part of the crusaders' debt if they would sack it; despite a papal letter forbidding the attack, the city was stormed and the loot shared—and it was here that Dandolo first encountered the fugitive Alexius Angelus.

summaries_by_episode.15: The city on the Dalmatian coast that Venice depended on for timber but which had come under the sway of the Christian King of Hungary. Dandolo offered to forgive part of the crusaders' debt if they would sack it; despite a papal letter forbidding the attack, the city was stormed and the loot shared—and it was here that Dandolo first encountered the fugitive Alexius Angelus.

places.name: Zepetra
places.summary: An important fortress captured alongside Samosata by Basil's brother during the sustained campaign against the Saracens.

summaries_by_episode.12: An important fortress captured alongside Samosata by Basil's brother during the sustained campaign against the Saracens.

events.name: Praetorian Guard Auction of the Throne (193)
events.summary: In 193, the praetorian guard auctioned off the office of emperor to the highest bidder, then killed the lucky winner 66 days later when he couldn't live up to his promises. Brownworth cites this as the starkest example of how the army had discovered the dangerous secret of empire.

summaries_by_episode.2: In 193, the praetorian guard auctioned off the office of emperor to the highest bidder, then killed the lucky winner 66 days later when he couldn't live up to his promises. Brownworth cites this as the starkest example of how the army had discovered the dangerous secret of empire.

events.name: Crisis of the Third Century
events.summary: The 50-year period between 235 and 285 that Brownworth calls the low point of Roman history, with emperors lasting an average of two years each. A 50-year-old in 285 would have known 27 legitimate emperors, numerous pretenders, and at least one breakaway empire.

summaries_by_episode.2: The 50-year period between 235 and 285 that Brownworth calls the low point of Roman history, with emperors lasting an average of two years each. A 50-year-old in 285 would have known 27 legitimate emperors, numerous pretenders, and at least one breakaway empire.

events.name: Roman–Persian War (283)
events.summary: Carus's eastern campaign that successfully sacked the Persian capital but ended abruptly on the banks of the Tigris when Carus died — officially by lightning, but Brownworth implies assassination by Diocletian. The army then began a long withdrawal through Asia Minor under Numerian.

summaries_by_episode.2: Carus's eastern campaign that successfully sacked the Persian capital but ended abruptly on the banks of the Tigris when Carus died — officially by lightning, but Brownworth implies assassination by Diocletian. The army then began a long withdrawal through Asia Minor under Numerian.

events.name: Death of Numerian
events.summary: Numerian, traveling in a closed carriage due to an eye infection, was found dead and partially decomposed when the army reached Nicomedia. Diocletian immediately accused Aper, publicly executed him on the spot, and was acclaimed emperor by the troops.

summaries_by_episode.2: Numerian, traveling in a closed carriage due to an eye infection, was found dead and partially decomposed when the army reached Nicomedia. Diocletian immediately accused Aper, publicly executed him on the spot, and was acclaimed emperor by the troops.

events.name: Diocletian's Divine Association (Jovian identity)
events.summary: Diocletian announced that he was the son of Jupiter, requiring all citizens to sacrifice to him and making assassination tantamount to sacrilege. He assigned Maximian to the junior deity Hercules to reinforce his own supremacy, and this policy brought him into direct conflict with Christians who refused to worship him.

summaries_by_episode.2: Diocletian announced that he was the son of Jupiter, requiring all citizens to sacrifice to him and making assassination tantamount to sacrilege. He assigned Maximian to the junior deity Hercules to reinforce his own supremacy, and this policy brought him into direct conflict with Christians who refused to worship him.

events.name: Battle of the River Margus
events.summary: The decisive confrontation between Diocletian and the western emperor Carinus near modern-day Belgrade. Carinus held the superior army and was on the verge of victory when one of his own officers, whose wife he had seduced, killed him, allowing Diocletian to become undisputed master of the Roman world.

summaries_by_episode.2: The decisive confrontation between Diocletian and the western emperor Carinus near modern-day Belgrade. Carinus held the superior army and was on the verge of victory when one of his own officers, whose wife he had seduced, killed him, allowing Diocletian to become undisputed master of the Roman world.

events.name: Division of the Roman Empire
events.summary: Brownworth describes Diocletian splitting the Roman Empire into eastern and western halves in 285 AD, with Constantinople serving as capital of the eastern half. He lists this as one of three candidate starting points for Byzantine history and ultimately chooses it as his own preferred beginning.

summaries_by_episode.1: Brownworth describes Diocletian splitting the Roman Empire into eastern and western halves in 285 AD, with Constantinople serving as capital of the eastern half. He lists this as one of three candidate starting points for Byzantine history and ultimately chooses it as his own preferred beginning.

events.name: Maximian Elevated to Augustus
events.summary: After Maximian crushed the Gaulish revolt but failed to cross to Britain due to its nascent fleet, Diocletian recognized the seriousness of the western situation and elevated Maximian from Caesar to the full rank of Augustus. Brownworth calls this an extraordinary development: the first peaceful division of the empire.

summaries_by_episode.2: After Maximian crushed the Gaulish revolt but failed to cross to Britain due to its nascent fleet, Diocletian recognized the seriousness of the western situation and elevated Maximian from Caesar to the full rank of Augustus. Brownworth calls this an extraordinary development: the first peaceful division of the empire.

events.name: Establishment of the Tetrarchy
events.summary: Diocletian and Maximian adopted their respective sons-in-law Galerius and Constantius Chlorus, raising them to the rank of Caesar to create a four-man ruling system. Brownworth notes the system is called the tetrarchy (four rulers), though he says the name is somewhat imprecise since it comprised two senior Augusti and two junior Caesars.

summaries_by_episode.2: Diocletian and Maximian adopted their respective sons-in-law Galerius and Constantius Chlorus, raising them to the rank of Caesar to create a four-man ruling system. Brownworth notes the system is called the tetrarchy (four rulers), though he says the name is somewhat imprecise since it comprised two senior Augusti and two junior Caesars.
summaries_by_episode.3: Diocletian's system of four co-rulers — two senior Augusti and two junior Caesars — intended to usher in an age of peace and prosperity. Instead, Brownworth says, it degenerated into full-blown civil war with up to four sides at once. Constantine was born into this confusing world.

events.summary_synthesized: Established by Diocletian, the Tetrarchy was a system of four co-rulers comprising two senior Augusti and two junior Caesars, created when Diocletian and Maximian adopted their sons-in-law Galerius and Constantius Chlorus and elevated them to the rank of Caesar. The arrangement was intended to bring stability and orderly succession to the Roman Empire. Instead, it collapsed into civil war involving multiple competing factions, the turmoil of which shaped the early life of Constantine.

events.name: Great Persecution of the Christians
events.summary: The last and most severe Roman persecution of Christians, which Brownworth says began as a direct consequence of Diocletian's policy of demanding divine worship from every citizen of the empire. Christians who refused to sacrifice to the emperor created a significant conflict.

summaries_by_episode.2: The last and most severe Roman persecution of Christians, which Brownworth says began as a direct consequence of Diocletian's policy of demanding divine worship from every citizen of the empire. Christians who refused to sacrifice to the emperor created a significant conflict.

events.name: Diocletian's Illness (304)
events.summary: During the celebration of his twentieth year in power in Rome, Diocletian fell deathly ill. He traveled toward Ravenna to escape the marshy climate but collapsed on the way, yet unexpectedly survived. Brownworth credits this illness with prompting Diocletian's decision to retire.

summaries_by_episode.2: During the celebration of his twentieth year in power in Rome, Diocletian fell deathly ill. He traveled toward Ravenna to escape the marshy climate but collapsed on the way, yet unexpectedly survived. Brownworth credits this illness with prompting Diocletian's decision to retire.

events.name: Abdication of Diocletian and Maximian
events.summary: On May 1st, 305, both Diocletian and a very reluctant Maximian simultaneously abdicated, elevating their Caesars to Augusti and establishing a new cycle of succession. Brownworth calls this Diocletian's most surprising decision and his most enduring legacy — the voluntary retirement of an emperor was virtually unprecedented.

summaries_by_episode.2: On May 1st, 305, both Diocletian and a very reluctant Maximian simultaneously abdicated, elevating their Caesars to Augusti and establishing a new cycle of succession. Brownworth calls this Diocletian's most surprising decision and his most enduring legacy — the voluntary retirement of an emperor was virtually unprecedented.

events.name: Abdication of Diocletian and Maximian
events.summary: Both senior Augusti abdicated, appointing Galerius and Constantius as the new senior emperors and Maximinus Daia and Severus as the new Caesars. The fatal flaw, Brownworth notes, was that the plan ignored the ambitious sons of Constantius and Maximian who were passed over.

summaries_by_episode.3: Both senior Augusti abdicated, appointing Galerius and Constantius as the new senior emperors and Maximinus Daia and Severus as the new Caesars. The fatal flaw, Brownworth notes, was that the plan ignored the ambitious sons of Constantius and Maximian who were passed over.

events.name: Campaign Against the Picts (305)
events.summary: Constantine joined his father Constantius Chlorus in Britain to fight the Picts in 305. They were successful together, and Constantius earned the title Britannicus Maximus, but he was too sick to enjoy it and died the following spring in York.

summaries_by_episode.3: Constantine joined his father Constantius Chlorus in Britain to fight the Picts in 305. They were successful together, and Constantius earned the title Britannicus Maximus, but he was too sick to enjoy it and died the following spring in York.

events.name: Army Proclaims Constantine Emperor
events.summary: After Constantius's death in York, his troops hailed Constantine as emperor without waiting for instructions. Galerius was furious but the deed was done; he mitigated the damage by confirming Constantine only as Caesar, not Augustus.

summaries_by_episode.3: After Constantius's death in York, his troops hailed Constantine as emperor without waiting for instructions. Galerius was furious but the deed was done; he mitigated the damage by confirming Constantine only as Caesar, not Augustus.

events.name: Death of Constantius Chlorus
events.summary: Constantius Chlorus died in York in the spring of 306, leaving Constantine without a legitimate claim to power. The army, which had already been won over by Constantine, hailed him as emperor without waiting for instructions from anyone, setting off a new round of civil conflict.

summaries_by_episode.3: Constantius Chlorus died in York in the spring of 306, leaving Constantine without a legitimate claim to power. The army, which had already been won over by Constantine, hailed him as emperor without waiting for instructions from anyone, setting off a new round of civil conflict.

events.name: Maxentius Seizes Rome
events.summary: Maxentius, also passed over in the Tetrarchic succession, moved into Rome, declared it tax-free, and was proclaimed emperor to cheering crowds. He then recalled his father Maximian as co-emperor and allied with Constantine by giving him his sister Fausta in marriage.

summaries_by_episode.3: Maxentius, also passed over in the Tetrarchic succession, moved into Rome, declared it tax-free, and was proclaimed emperor to cheering crowds. He then recalled his father Maximian as co-emperor and allied with Constantine by giving him his sister Fausta in marriage.

events.name: Defeat and Death of Severus
events.summary: Galerius sent Severus to crush Maxentius in Italy, but Severus's army melted away at the very walls of Rome due to massive, well-placed bribes from Maxentius. Severus fled to Ravenna, was captured, forced to abdicate, and killed.

summaries_by_episode.3: Galerius sent Severus to crush Maxentius in Italy, but Severus's army melted away at the very walls of Rome due to massive, well-placed bribes from Maxentius. Severus fled to Ravenna, was captured, forced to abdicate, and killed.

events.name: Galerius Invades Italy
events.summary: Galerius, deciding to handle the matter himself, invaded Italy. His army proved just as susceptible to Maxentius's bribes, and he barely escaped Italy alive.

summaries_by_episode.3: Galerius, deciding to handle the matter himself, invaded Italy. His army proved just as susceptible to Maxentius's bribes, and he barely escaped Italy alive.

events.name: Conference of Carnuntum
events.summary: The emperors came to Diocletian in 308 to beg him to return to power as the empire had fallen into chaos after his retirement. Diocletian refused, famously replying that if they could see the cabbages he had planted with his own hands at Salona, they would never again judge the empire a tempting prospect.

summaries_by_episode.2: The emperors came to Diocletian in 308 to beg him to return to power as the empire had fallen into chaos after his retirement. Diocletian refused, famously replying that if they could see the cabbages he had planted with his own hands at Salona, they would never again judge the empire a tempting prospect.

events.name: Diocletian Called Out of Retirement
events.summary: The surviving emperors called Diocletian out of retirement to stabilize the situation. His solution was a new official Tetrarchy, with Licinius as the new western Augustus, and Constantine confirmed as Caesar. It did not survive the year.

summaries_by_episode.3: The surviving emperors called Diocletian out of retirement to stabilize the situation. His solution was a new official Tetrarchy, with Licinius as the new western Augustus, and Constantine confirmed as Caesar. It did not survive the year.

events.name: Maximian Attempts to Overthrow Maxentius
events.summary: Maximian, probably jealous at having to share power with his son, attempted to overthrow Maxentius. He botched the attempt and fled to his son-in-law Constantine.

summaries_by_episode.3: Maximian, probably jealous at having to share power with his son, attempted to overthrow Maxentius. He botched the attempt and fled to his son-in-law Constantine.

events.name: Maximian's Forced Suicide
events.summary: Taking advantage of Constantine's absence, Maximian declared him dead and proclaimed himself emperor. Constantine was very much alive and pursued him; Maximian fled to Marseilles, but the city opened its gates to Constantine, and Maximian was forced to commit suicide. Brownworth notes the killing of his father-in-law affected Constantine deeply and drew him toward monotheism.

summaries_by_episode.3: Taking advantage of Constantine's absence, Maximian declared him dead and proclaimed himself emperor. Constantine was very much alive and pursued him; Maximian fled to Marseilles, but the city opened its gates to Constantine, and Maximian was forced to commit suicide. Brownworth notes the killing of his father-in-law affected Constantine deeply and drew him toward monotheism.

events.name: Death of Diocletian
events.summary: Diocletian died in 311 as a contented yet disillusioned private citizen. His daughter and wife had been exiled by his successors and his statues torn down, yet his abdication so awed contemporaries that one wrote he was unique in all human history: he died a private citizen but was still enrolled among the gods.

summaries_by_episode.2: Diocletian died in 311 as a contented yet disillusioned private citizen. His daughter and wife had been exiled by his successors and his statues torn down, yet his abdication so awed contemporaries that one wrote he was unique in all human history: he died a private citizen but was still enrolled among the gods.

events.name: Death of Galerius
events.summary: Galerius, by then hopelessly obese and dying from a horrendous cancer, suspended all persecutions of Christians and ordered churches built and prayers said on his behalf, but did not survive long enough to hear them. His death triggered a showdown between Licinius and Maximinus Daia.

summaries_by_episode.3: Galerius, by then hopelessly obese and dying from a horrendous cancer, suspended all persecutions of Christians and ordered churches built and prayers said on his behalf, but did not survive long enough to hear them. His death triggered a showdown between Licinius and Maximinus Daia.

events.name: Battle of the Milvian Bridge
events.summary: Constantine routed Maxentius at Saxa Rubra and chased him back to the Milvian Bridge on October 28, 312. Maxentius's pontoon bridge collapsed when engineers pulled the bolts too early; the panicked army was crushed or thrown off the old Milvian Bridge. Maxentius drowned in the Tiber. Brownworth calls this 'a watershed moment in history,' marking the beginning of Constantine's active protection of Christianity.

summaries_by_episode.3: Constantine routed Maxentius at Saxa Rubra and chased him back to the Milvian Bridge on October 28, 312. Maxentius's pontoon bridge collapsed when engineers pulled the bolts too early; the panicked army was crushed or thrown off the old Milvian Bridge. Maxentius drowned in the Tiber. Brownworth calls this 'a watershed moment in history,' marking the beginning of Constantine's active protection of Christianity.

events.name: Constantine Crosses the Alps
events.summary: In 312, Constantine crossed the Alps and invaded Italy, shrewdly having concluded that Maxentius's territory technically belonged to Licinius and he could keep it for himself. He sacked Turin and Verona before appearing outside Rome on October 28th.

summaries_by_episode.3: In 312, Constantine crossed the Alps and invaded Italy, shrewdly having concluded that Maxentius's territory technically belonged to Licinius and he could keep it for himself. He sacked Turin and Verona before appearing outside Rome on October 28th.

events.name: Constantine's Vision at Saxa Rubra
events.summary: At Saxa Rubra (Red Rocks), seven or eight miles northeast of Rome, Constantine had his famous vision. According to historian Lactantius, writing one or two years after the event, it consisted of a private dream telling Constantine to mark his soldiers with the chi-rho, the first two letters of Christ's name in Greek. Armed with a new certainty of divine favor, he routed Maxentius.

summaries_by_episode.3: At Saxa Rubra (Red Rocks), seven or eight miles northeast of Rome, Constantine had his famous vision. According to historian Lactantius, writing one or two years after the event, it consisted of a private dream telling Constantine to mark his soldiers with the chi-rho, the first two letters of Christ's name in Greek. Armed with a new certainty of divine favor, he routed Maxentius.

events.name: Edict of Milan
